The same gasoline vapour can deflagate in an open pan but detonate in a confined pipe. ### Common Industrial Explosion Types - **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE):** Release of flammable vapour into atmosphere followed by delayed ignition. - **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE):** Catastrophic failure of a pressurised liquefied gas vessel exposed to fire. - **Dust Explosion:** Combustion of finely divided combustible solids (grain, flour, coal, metal dust). - **Unconfined Vapour Cloud Explosion (UVCE):** A VCE in open atmosphere — e.g., Flixborough disaster, UK (1974). --- ## 1.3 Toxic Gas Release — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > A **toxic gas release** is the unintended escape of a harmful gaseous, vapour, or aerosol substance from its containment (vessel, pipeline, reactor) into the workplace or environment in quantities sufficient to cause **acute or chronic health effects** in exposed personnel. ### Key Exposure Metrics | Acronym | Full Form | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **TLV** | Threshold Limit Value | Airborne concentration below which most workers can be repeatedly exposed without adverse effect (8-hr TWA) | | **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit | OSHA-enforceable legal limit | | **IDLH** | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | Maximum concentration from which a worker can escape within 30 minutes without irreversible health effects | | **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration 50% | Concentration that kills 50% of test population | | **STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | 15-minute time-weighted average | ### Conceptual Analogy — The Leaking Perfume Bottle > [!NOTE] > Imagine spraying perfume in a closed room. Within seconds the smell (molecules) spreads everywhere. Now imagine that "perfume" is **chlorine gas** instead. The same diffusion physics applies — but the health consequence is death. **Toxic release analysis is precisely the engineering problem of predicting where those invisible molecules travel, and at what concentration.** --- ## 1.4 Summary Table — Distinguishing the Three Hazards | Parameter | Fire | Explosion | Toxic Release | |---|---|---|---| | Primary Energy | Chemical (combustion) | Mechanical (pressure) + Chemical | Toxicological | | Speed of Event | Seconds to hours | Milliseconds | Minutes to days | | Main Damage | Thermal, smoke | Overpressure, fragments | Chemical injury, asphyxiation | | Key Metric | Heat release rate (kW) | Peak overpressure (kPa) | Concentration × Time (ppm·hr) | | Detection | Smoke/heat detectors | Pressure sensors | Gas detectors, dosimeters | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fire Science Fundamentals ### 2.1.1 Flammability Characteristics of a Substance For every combustible material, four critical parameters determine fire/explosion behaviour: 1. **Flash Point (FP)** — Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapour to form an *ignitable* mixture near the surface (but not sustain combustion). 2. **Fire Point (FP)** — Lowest temperature at which sustained combustion occurs. 3. **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — Minimum temperature at which a substance ignites *spontaneously* without an external source. 4. **Flammability Limits** — Concentration range in air within which the mixture can propagate flame. ### 2.1.2 Flammability Limits — Lower & Upper - **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL / LEL):** Minimum fuel concentration in air that supports combustion. - **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL / UEL):** Maximum fuel concentration above which the mixture is "too rich" to burn. > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb (Le Chatelier's):** A mixture is flammable only if its composition lies **between LFL and UFL**. Outside this range, no amount of ignition energy will start a fire. ### 2.1.3 Limits of Inflammability (Crowl & Louvar Empirical) For pure substances at **25 °C and 1 atm**: $$ LFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y_i}{LFL_i}} $$ $$ UFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y_i}{UFL_i}} $$ Where: - $y_i$ = mole fraction of component *i* in the fuel mixture - $LFL_i, UFL_i$ = lower and upper flammable limits of pure component *i* ### 2.1.4 Adiabatic Flame Temperature The maximum theoretical temperature reached assuming no heat loss: $$ T_{ad} = T_0 + \frac{Y_f \cdot \Delta H_c}{C_p \cdot (1 + Y_f \cdot \rho_f / \rho_{air})} $$ Where $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg), $Y_f$ = fuel mass fraction, $C_p$ = specific heat. --- ## 2.2 Explosion Parameters ### 2.2.1 Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC) The minimum oxygen concentration below which combustion cannot occur: $$ LOC = \frac{LFL \cdot \sigma}{1 - LFL \cdot (1 - \sigma)} \cdot 100 $$ Where $\sigma$ is the inert gas / fuel stoichiometric ratio (typically taken from stoichiometry). ### 2.2.2 Minimum Explosive Concentration (MEC) for Dusts For dust clouds, MEC is analogous to LFL. Typical values: | Dust Type | MEC (g/m³) | |---|---| | Grain dust | 50 | | Coal dust | 35 | | Aluminium dust | 40 | | Sulphur | 35 | | Sugar | 60 | ### 2.2.3 TNT Equivalent Method Used to estimate damage from vapour-cloud and condensed-phase explosions. The energy released is converted to mass of TNT: $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}} $$ Where: - $\eta$ = **empirical yield factor** (typically 0.01–0.05 for VCE, 0.1–0.4 for condensed explosives) - $M$ = mass of fuel released (kg) - $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg) - $E_{TNT} = 4,680$ kJ/kg (energy of TNT) ### 2.2.4 Overpressure Damage Estimation (Cube-Root Scaling) The scaled distance determines damage: $$ Z = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} $$ Where $R$ = distance from explosion (m), $W_{TNT}$ = TNT mass (kg). | Overpressure (kPa) | Damage | |---|---| | 7 | Window breakage | | 15 | Roof damage | | 35 | Heavy structural damage | | 70 | Total destruction of buildings | | 200 | Lung rupture in humans | --- ## 2.3 Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model The **Pasquill-Gifford Gaussian Plume Model** is the cornerstone for predicting downwind concentration from a continuous point source (e.g., stack, leak): $$ C(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2 \sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2 \sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2 \sigma_z^2}\right)\right] $$ Where: - $C(x,y,z)$ = concentration at point (x,y,z) in **g/m³** or **ppm** - $Q$ = emission rate (g/s) - $u$ = mean wind speed at release height (m/s) - $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = horizontal & vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of x and stability class) - $H$ = effective release height (m) ### Ground-Level Concentration (y = 0, z = 0) At the centreline, on the ground: $$ C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2 \sigma_z^2}\right) $$ ### Pasquill Stability Classes | Class | Description | Conditions (Day/Night) | |---|---|---| | A | Very Unstable | Strong insolation, clear sky | | B | Unstable | Moderate insolation | | C | Slightly Unstable | Slight insolation | | D | Neutral | Overcast day or night | | E | Slightly Stable | Clear night, light wind | | F | Stable | Clear night, calm wind | --- ## 2.4 Toxicological Dose–Response (Probit Equations) The **Probit model** relates the probability of injury/death to the toxic load: $$ Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t) $$ Where $Y$ = probit variable, $C$ = concentration (ppm), $t$ = exposure time (min), and $a, b, n$ are substance-specific constants. **Common examples:** - **Chlorine (death):** $Y = -19.83 + 3.87 \ln(C^2 \cdot t)$ - **Ammonia (death):** $Y = -35.9 + 1.85 \ln(C^2 \cdot t)$ - **Hydrogen Sulphide (death):** $Y = -31.42 + 3.008 \ln(C^1.43 \cdot t)$ The percentage of population affected is read from a standard probit table. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Use Case | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $LFL_{mix}^{-1} = \sum (y_i / LFL_i)$ | Multi-component fuel mixture | mole frac | | 2 | $UFL_{mix}^{-1} = \sum (y_i / UFL_i)$ | Multi-component fuel mixture | mole frac | | 3 | $LOC = (LFL \cdot \sigma) / (1 - LFL \cdot (1 - \sigma))$ | Inertion design | % O₂ | | 4 | $W_{TNT} = \eta M \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$ | Explosion damage mapping | kg TNT | | 5 | $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ | Scaled distance | m / kg^(1/3) | | 6 | $C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp(-y^2/2\sigma_y^2) \cdot [\exp(-(z-H)^2/2\sigma_z^2) + \exp(-(z+H)^2/2\sigma_z^2)]$ | Continuous release dispersion | g/m³ | | 7 | $C_{max} = Q / (\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z) \cdot \exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ | Ground centreline concentration | g/m³ | | 8 | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ | Toxicological probit | dimensionless | | 9 | $T_{ad} = T_0 + Y_f \Delta H_c / [C_p (1 + Y_f \rho_f / \rho_{air})]$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | K | | 10 | $BLEVE \rightarrow$ fireball radius: $R_f = 2.9 W^{1/3}$ | Fireball sizing | m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas form the backbone of **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** in petroleum refineries, chemical plants, LNG terminals, and pharmaceutical facilities. KTU examiners consistently test the ability to *apply* these formulas, not just recall them. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Le Chatelier's Rule for Lower Flammability Limit ### 3.1.1 Statement For a mixture of multiple flammable gases, the lower flammable limit is determined by the volume fraction of each component. ### 3.1.2 Assumptions 1. The mixture is a homogeneous ideal gas. 2. Each component contributes its own flammability behaviour independently. 3. The thermal conductivity of the mixture is approximately equal to that of air. ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define mole fractions at LFL** At the LFL of a mixture, the total fuel concentration just equals the LFL of the mixture: $$ C_{mix, LFL} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i $$ where $C_i = y_i \cdot C_{mix, LFL}$ is the contribution from each component. **Step 2 — Apply the adiabatic flame temperature criterion** Each component alone reaches its LFL when the adiabatic flame temperature equals a critical value $T_{cr}$. For the mixture to be at its LFL, the same critical temperature must be reached. $$ T_{ad} = T_0 + \frac{\sum_i y_i \cdot C_{mix, LFL} \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}}{C_p} $$ **Step 3 — Equate to single-component LFL condition** For a single component $i$: $$ T_{cr} - T_0 = \frac{C_{LFL,i} \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}}{C_p} $$ So $C_{LFL,i} = C_p(T_{cr} - T_0)/\Delta H_{c,i}$. **Step 4 — Substitute into mixture expression** $$ C_p (T_{cr} - T_0) = \sum_i y_i \cdot C_{mix, LFL} \cdot \Delta H_{c,i} $$ Dividing both sides by $C_{mix, LFL}$: $$ \frac{C_p(T_{cr} - T_0)}{C_{mix, LFL}} = \sum_i y_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i} $$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of $LFL_i$** Since $C_p(T_{cr} - T_0) = C_{LFL,i} \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}$: $$ \sum_i \frac{C_{mix, LFL} \cdot y_i}{C_{LFL,i}} = 1 $$ **Step 6 — Solve for $LFL_{mix}$** $$ \boxed{LFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y_i}{LFL_i}}} $$ **Conversion logic:** The energy released by the mixture at LFL must equal the critical combustion energy. By equating the energy per unit volume of air for the mixture to the same quantity for each pure component, the additive inverse form emerges naturally. > [!NOTE] > The **UFL** formula has an identical form but with different empirical constants. It is derived analogously using the rich-mixture critical temperature. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Ground-Level Centreline Concentration (Gaussian Plume) ### 3.2.1 Starting Equation $$ C(x, y, z) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right] $$ ### 3.2.2 Apply Conditions **Condition 1 — Centreline:** $y = 0$ Then $\exp(-y^2 / 2\sigma_y^2) = \exp(0) = 1$. **Condition 2 — Ground-level receptor:** $z = 0$ $$ (z - H)^2 = H^2 \quad \text{and} \quad (z + H)^2 = H^2 $$ So both exponential terms become $\exp(-H^2 / 2\sigma_z^2)$. ### 3.2.3 Simplification $$ C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot 1 \cdot 2 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) $$ $$ \boxed{C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)} $$ **Conversion logic:** The "image source" technique assumes the ground acts as a perfect reflector for the plume. Mathematically this duplicates the source at depth $H$ below the ground, and the two contributions superimpose at $z=0$, giving the factor of 2. ### 3.2.4 Maximum Concentration To find $x_{max}$, differentiate $C$ w.r.t. $x$ and set to zero. For typical dispersion tables, $x_{max}$ occurs where $\sigma_z(x) = H/\sqrt{2}$. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem: TNT Equivalent & Damage Distance > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A storage vessel ruptures and releases **10,000 kg of propane** which forms a vapour cloud and explodes. Using the **TNT equivalent method**, determine: > (a) TNT equivalent mass (yield factor $\eta = 0.05$, $\Delta H_c$ of propane = 46,000 kJ/kg). > (b) The distance at which a peak overpressure of **35 kPa** is expected. > (c) Damage expected at that distance. ### Step 1 — Compute TNT Equivalent Mass Given: - $M = 10{,}000$ kg - $\eta = 0.05$ - $\Delta H_c = 46{,}000$ kJ/kg - $E_{TNT} = 4{,}680$ kJ/kg $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}} $$ $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{0.05 \times 10{,}000 \times 46{,}000}{4{,}680} $$ $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{23{,}000{,}000}{4{,}680} \approx 4{,}914.5 \text{ kg TNT} $$ ### Step 2 — Find Scaled Distance for 35 kPa Overpressure From empirical TNT blast charts, peak side-on overpressure $\Delta P = 35$ kPa corresponds to **scaled distance $Z = 3.0$ m/kg^(1/3)**. ### Step 3 — Compute Physical Distance $$ R = Z \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3} $$ $$ R = 3.0 \times (4{,}914.5)^{1/3} $$ $$ R = 3.0 \times 16.98 \approx 50.9 \text{ m} $$ ### Step 4 — Interpret Damage At **35 kPa overpressure** at ~**51 m**, we expect: - Heavy structural damage to buildings - Most brick walls collapse - Storage tanks deformed - Severe injury risk to personnel outdoors > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to take the cube root in the scaled-distance formula. Another frequent error is using $E_{TNT} = 4{,}680$ kJ/kg but accidentally applying it to gram units — keep all units in **kg and kJ**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem: Gaussian Plume Concentration **Problem:** A chlorine plant releases **Q = 100 g/s** of Cl₂ from a height **H = 30 m**. Wind speed **u = 4 m/s**, stability class **D**. Find the ground-level centreline concentration at **downwind distance x = 1 km**. ### Step 1 — Look Up Dispersion Coefficients (Pasquill-Gifford, Class D) For $x = 1000$ m, Class D: - $\sigma_y \approx 33$ m - $\sigma_z \approx 18$ m ### Step 2 — Apply Ground-Level Centreline Equation $$ C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Values $$ C = \frac{100}{\pi \times 4 \times 33 \times 18} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{30^2}{2 \times 18^2}\right) $$ Compute denominator: $\pi \times 4 \times 33 \times 18 \approx 7{,}463.4$ Compute exponent: $\exp(-900 / 648) = \exp(-1.389) \approx 0.2493$ $$ C \approx \frac{100}{7463.4} \times 0.2493 \approx 0.0134 \times 0.2493 $$ $$ C \approx 0.00334 \text{ g/m}^3 = 3.34 \text{ mg/m}^3 \approx 1.10 \text{ ppm} $$ ### Step 4 — Compare with Toxicological Limits - **TLV-TWA for Cl₂:** 1 ppm → concentration exceeded → unsafe for chronic exposure. - **AEGL-1 (10 min):** 0.5 ppm → exceeded. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the $\exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ term, dramatically overestimating ground-level concentration for tall stacks. Also, never confuse **g/m³** with **ppm** — for Cl₂ (MW = 71), 1 ppm ≈ 2.9 mg/m³. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fire Triangle & Tetrahedron ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FireTet [FIRE TETRAHEDRON - Four Pillars] nodeA[FUEL
Combustible Material] nodeB[OXIDIZER
Oxygen in Air] nodeC[IGNITION
Heat Source] nodeD[CHAIN REACTION
Free Radical Propagation] end nodeA --- nodeB nodeA --- nodeC nodeB --- nodeC nodeD --- nodeA nodeD --- nodeB nodeD --- nodeC style nodeA fill:#FF6B35,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style nodeB fill:#004E89,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style nodeC fill:#FFD23F,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#9B5DE5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - The classical **fire triangle** is the inner triangle of nodes A, B, C. - The modern **fire tetrahedron** adds node D — the uninhibited chain reaction of free radicals. - Removing any one node **extinguishes** the fire — this is the foundation of all fire suppression strategies. --- ## 4.2 Event Tree for BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeS1[Storage Vessel
Contains LPG under
Pressure and Heat] nodeS2{Heat from
External Fire?} nodeS3[Vessel Shell
Temperature Rises] nodeS4{PRV Opens
and Functions?} nodeS5[Pressure Relief
Successful] nodeS6[Pressure Builds
Beyond Critical] nodeS7{Mechanical
Strength Lost?} nodeS8[Catastrophic
Vessel Failure] nodeS9[BLEVE Event
Fireball and
Fragment Projection] nodeS10[Safe Venting
Scenario] nodeS1 --> nodeS2 nodeS2 -- Yes --> nodeS3 nodeS3 --> nodeS4 nodeS4 -- Yes --> nodeS5 nodeS5 --> nodeS10 nodeS4 -- No --> nodeS6 nodeS6 --> nodeS7 nodeS7 -- Yes --> nodeS8 nodeS8 --> nodeS9 nodeS7 -- No --> nodeS10 style nodeS9 fill:#E63946,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF style nodeS10 fill:#06A77D,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - A BLEVE requires **three failures in sequence**: (1) external fire exposure, (2) PRV failure or insufficient capacity, (3) shell weakening. - The single catastrophic outcome is a fireball with **fragment projection up to 500 m**. - Prevention focuses on water cooling, PRV sizing, and **removal of surrounding fuel/ignition sources**. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Quantitative Risk Assessment Workflow for Toxic Release ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Stage1 [STAGE 1 - Source Modelling] step1A[Identify Hazardous
Materials and Inventory] step1B[Determine Release
Scenarios: Pump, Flange,
Vessel, Pipe] step1C[Estimate Release
Rate Q kg per s] end subgraph Stage2 [STAGE 2 - Dispersion Modelling] step2A[Select Atmospheric
Stability Class A-F] step2B[Compute Wind
Speed u and Direction] step2C[Apply Gaussian Plume
Model C of x y z] end subgraph Stage3 [STAGE 3 - Consequence Analysis] step3A[Compare Concentration
with TLV and IDLH] step3B[Compute Toxic Load
via Probit Equation] step3C[Estimate Affected
Population and Area] end subgraph Stage4 [STAGE 4 - Risk Characterisation] step4A[Determine Individual
Risk per year] step4B[Determine Societal
Risk F-N Curve] step4C[Compare with
Acceptance Criteria] end step1A --> step1B --> step1C step1C --> step2A --> step2B --> step2C step2C --> step3A --> step3B --> step3C step3C --> step4A --> step4B --> step4C style Stage1 fill:#F4A261,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Stage2 fill:#2A9D8F,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style Stage3 fill:#E76F51,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style Stage4 fill:#264653,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - A complete QRA follows a **4-stage pipeline**: source → dispersion → consequence → risk. - Each block represents a **mathematical or engineering procedure** with corresponding formulas from Section 2. - The final output feeds emergency planning, land-use zoning, and insurance decisions. --- ## 4.4 Explosion Damage Zone Classification ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ZoneCenter [BLAST ORIGIN - R less than 50 m] zA[Zone 1
Lethal Zone
Overpressure greater than 200 kPa
Total Destruction] end subgraph ZoneMid [INTERMEDIATE - R 50 to 200 m] zB[Zone 2
Heavy Damage Zone
Overpressure 35 to 70 kPa
Building Collapse] end subgraph ZoneOut [OUTER - R 200 to 500 m] zC[Zone 3
Moderate Damage Zone
Overpressure 7 to 15 kPa
Window Breakage and Injuries] end subgraph ZoneSafe [SAFE - R greater than 500 m] zD[Zone 4
Negligible Damage
Overpressure less than 7 kPa] end zA --> zB --> zC --> zD style zA fill:#D62828,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style zB fill:#F77F00,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style zC fill:#FCBF49,stroke:#000,color:#000 style zD fill:#06A77D,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - This zoning is based on the **TNT cube-root scaling** methodology. - Plant layouts must respect a **minimum safety distance** to keep occupied buildings outside Zone 1 and Zone 2. - KTU exam questions often test the ability to calculate these distances for a given fuel mass. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Model Question 1]** > > **Q1.** Define the **Fire Triangle**. List the three elements and explain how removing any one extinguishes a fire. **Model Answer:** The Fire Triangle represents the three necessary conditions for combustion: 1. **Fuel** — any combustible material (solid, liquid, or gas). 2. **Oxidizer** — typically atmospheric oxygen (≥ LOC for that fuel). 3. **Ignition Source** — heat energy exceeding the auto-ignition temperature or minimum ignition energy. Fire extinguishment works by removing one leg: - **Water cooling** → removes heat from fuel → reduces below AIT. - **Foam blanketing** → physically separates fuel from air. - **CO₂ flooding** → dilutes oxygen below the LOC. - **Dry chemical** → breaks the chain reaction (acts on tetrahedron's 4th leg). **(Marks: 1 – definition, 1 – three elements, 1 – extinguishment mechanism.)** --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Model Question 2]** > > **Q2.** Differentiate between **deflagration and detonation** with suitable examples. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Deflagration | Detonation | |---|---|---| | Flame speed | Subsonic (< 340 m/s) | Supersonic (1,800–9,000 m/s) | | Pressure rise | Gradual | Sudden shock front | | Mechanism | Heat conduction | Shock compression | | Damage type | Thermal | Overpressure + fragmentation | | Example | Gas burner flame, pool fire | Dynamite explosion, vapour cloud detonation | **(Marks: 1.5 – clear distinction, 1.5 – examples.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Module 4 | 14 Marks]** > > **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Fire Tetrahedron**. Describe how it improves upon the classical fire triangle. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A leak from a chlorine storage facility releases **Q = 250 g/s** of Cl₂ continuously. The release height is **H = 20 m** and wind speed is **u = 5 m/s** under Pasquill stability class **D**. Using the Gaussian Plume Model, calculate the **ground-level centreline concentration at x = 800 m**. Comment on the safety implications given that the TLV-TWA for chlorine is **1 ppm**. **(7 Marks)** ### Solution to A(a) The Fire Tetrahedron extends the classical Fire Triangle by adding a fourth element: the **uninhibited chain reaction** of free radicals (H•, OH•, O•). **Four pillars:** 1. **Fuel** — Combustible substance providing carbon/hydrogen. 2. **Oxidizer** — Oxygen (or other oxidising agent). 3. **Heat (Ignition Source)** — Energy sufficient to initiate combustion. 4. **Chain Reaction** — Self-sustaining propagation of free radicals in the flame zone. **Improvement over Fire Triangle:** - The classical triangle explains *initiation* but not *self-sustenance*. - Many fires (e.g., liquid pool fires) cannot be extinguished by cooling alone — the chain reaction continues. Dry chemical and halogenated agents specifically interrupt the chain reaction, which the triangle does not represent. - The tetrahedron is the modern scientific basis for selection of fire suppression media. **Sketch (textual representation):** ``` [HEAT] / \ / \ [FUEL]----[OXYGEN] \ / \ / [CHAIN RXN] ``` **(Marks: 2 – diagram, 2 – four elements with explanation, 3 – improvement over triangle.)** --- ### Solution to A(b) **Given:** - Q = 250 g/s - H = 20 m - u = 5 m/s - x = 800 m - Stability class D **Step 1 — Look up Pasquill-Gifford coefficients at x = 800 m, Class D:** - $\sigma_y \approx 28$ m - $\sigma_z \approx 14$ m **Step 2 — Apply the ground-level centreline equation:** $$ C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2 \sigma_z^2}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** Denominator: $\pi \times 5 \times 28 \times 14 \approx 6{,}157.5$ Exponent argument: $-H^2 / (2\sigma_z^2) = -400 / (2 \times 196) = -400 / 392 \approx -1.0204$ Exponential term: $\exp(-1.0204) \approx 0.3603$ **Step 4 — Calculate concentration:** $$ C = \frac{250}{6157.5} \times 0.3603 \approx 0.0406 \times 0.3603 \approx 0.01463 \text{ g/m}^3 $$ **Step 5 — Convert to ppm:** For Cl₂, MW = 71 g/mol. Using ideal gas at 25°C, 1 atm: $$ 1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{71}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3 \approx 2.9 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$ $$ C = 14.63 \text{ mg/m}^3 \approx \frac{14.63}{2.9} \approx 5.04 \text{ ppm} $$ **Step 6 — Safety Comment:** Since **5.04 ppm > 1 ppm (TLV-TWA)**, the concentration at 800 m downwind exceeds the permissible chronic exposure limit. Furthermore, AEGL-1 (10 min) for chlorine is 0.5 ppm and AEGL-2 is 2.8 ppm — both exceeded. This indicates that **emergency response plans must include evacuation of personnel within an 800 m radius** and deployment of full chemical protective equipment for response teams. **(Marks: 1.5 – formula, 2 – substitution and calculation, 1.5 – conversion to ppm, 2 – safety interpretation.)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Module 4 | 14 Marks]** > > **(a)** What is a **BLEVE**? Explain with a labelled diagram the sequence of events leading to a BLEVE. List **four major preventive measures**. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A refinery accident releases **15,000 kg of cyclohexane** which forms a vapour cloud and explodes. Compute the **TNT equivalent mass** assuming a yield factor of 0.04. Heat of combustion of cyclohexane = **46,800 kJ/kg**. If the cloud detonates, find the **distance at which 70 kPa overpressure is expected**. **(7 Marks)** ### Solution to B(a) **BLEVE — Definition:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion)** is the catastrophic failure of a pressure vessel containing a liquefied gas when subjected to external fire heating. The liquid boils rapidly, the vessel ruptures, and a fraction of the contents flash-instantly to vapour, producing a massive fireball and projectile fragments. **Sequence of Events (with reference to event tree in Section 4.2):** 1. External fire impinges on the vessel shell (e.g., LPG bullet, propane road tanker). 2. Shell metal temperature rises; if shell material is above critical weakening temperature (~ **650 °C for carbon steel**), strength drops sharply. 3. Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) activates but may be undersized or blocked. 4. Internal pressure rises until the vessel's **burst pressure** is exceeded. 5. Vessel fails catastrophically — the sudden depressurisation causes the superheated liquid to **flash to vapour** (multiplication factor up to 250:1 for propane). 6. The vapour mixes with air, is ignited by the external fire, and forms a **fireball**. 7. Fragments are ejected ballistically — often 200–500 m. **Four Preventive Measures:** 1. **Water spray (deluge) cooling** on vessel exterior — keeps shell below 100 °C. 2. **Adequate PRV sizing** for fire case (per API 521). 3. **Thermal insulation** on storage vessels. 4. **Removal of surrounding combustible material** and **firebreaks** between vessels. 5. (Bonus) **Bund walls** to contain spills; **gas detection systems**. **(Marks: 2 – definition, 2 – sequence, 3 – four preventive measures.)** --- ### Solution to B(b) **Given:** - $M = 15{,}000$ kg - $\eta = 0.04$ - $\Delta H_c = 46{,}800$ kJ/kg - $E_{TNT} = 4{,}680$ kJ/kg - Target $\Delta P = 70$ kPa **Step 1 — Compute TNT Equivalent Mass:** $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}} $$ $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{0.04 \times 15{,}000 \times 46{,}800}{4{,}680} $$ $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{28{,}080{,}000}{4{,}680} \approx 6{,}000 \text{ kg TNT} $$ **Step 2 — Find Scaled Distance for 70 kPa:** From TNT blast curves, $\Delta P = 70$ kPa corresponds to **scaled distance Z ≈ 1.9 m/kg^(1/3)**. **Step 3 — Compute Physical Distance:** $$ R = Z \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 1.9 \times (6{,}000)^{1/3} $$ $$ R = 1.9 \times 18.17 \approx 34.5 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** At ~**34.5 m**, peak overpressure is **70 kPa** — equivalent to total destruction of buildings. Critical equipment and occupied control rooms must be placed beyond this radius. **(Marks: 2 – TNT formula, 2 – substitution and result, 1.5 – scaled distance, 1.5 – physical distance and interpretation.)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common reasons students LOSE marks in Module 4 questions:** > > 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing g/m³ with ppm without proper conversion using molecular weight. Always state the conversion explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the stack height term** $\exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ in Gaussian plume calculations — this term is mandatory even when the question doesn't mention it. > 3. **Wrong yield factor:** Students use $\eta = 1$ for VCE which is unphysical. Always use 0.01–0.05 for vapour cloud explosions unless explicitly told otherwise. > 4. **Cube-root scaling error:** Forgetting to take the cube root of TNT mass before multiplying by Z. This single mistake can shift your answer by an order of magnitude. > 5. **Confusing LFL/UFL with LOC:** These are three different parameters. LFL/UFL apply to fuel concentration in air; LOC applies to oxygen concentration. > 6. **Sketch quality:** Free-hand sketches with no labels fetch partial credit only. Always label **all** arrows and components. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Fire, Explosion & Toxic Gas Release** ### Fire Essentials - 🔥 **Fire Triangle**: Fuel + Oxidizer + Ignition Source - 🔥 **Fire Tetrahedron**: Adds Chain Reaction (free radicals) - 🔥 **LFL/UFL**: Flammability range of fuel in air - 🔥 **LOC**: Limiting Oxygen Concentration (inertion target) - 🔥 **Flash Point vs Fire Point vs AIT**: Three temperature milestones - 🔥 **Adiabatic Flame Temperature**: $T_{ad}$ maximum theoretical - 🔥 **Le Chatelier's Rule**: $LFL_{mix}^{-1} = \sum (y_i/LFL_i)$ ### Explosion Essentials - 💥 **Deflagration vs Detonation**: Subsonic vs supersonic - 💥 **BLEVE**: Vessel rupture under external fire - 💥 **VCE / UVCE / Dust explosion**: Three industrial categories - 💥 **TNT Equivalent**: $W_{TNT} = \eta M \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$ - 💥 **Cube-root scaling**: $R = Z \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ - 💥 **Damage zones**: 7 kPa windows, 35 kPa walls, 70 kPa total destruction ### Toxic Release Essentials - ☣️ **TLV, PEL, IDLH, LC₅₀, STEL**: Exposure metrics - ☣️ **Gaussian Plume Equation**: The fundamental dispersion formula - ☣️ **Pasquill Stability Classes**: A (very unstable) → F (stable) - ☣️ **Ground centreline**: $C = (Q/\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z) \cdot \exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ - ☣️ **Probit equation**: $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ - ☣️ **Unit conversion**: 1 ppm = MW/24.45 mg/m³ at 25°C ### Engineering Best Practices - 🛡️ **Inertion**: Reduce O₂ below LOC - 🛡️ **Ventilation**: Dilute below LFL - 🛡️ **Containment**: Bund walls, dykes - 🛡️ **Detection**: Smoke, heat, gas detectors - 🛡️ **Suppression**: Water spray, foam, dry chemical, CO₂ - 🛡️ **Separation**: Maintain safety distances per TNT scaling ### Final Numerical Constants to Memorise - 🔑 $E_{TNT} = 4{,}680$ kJ/kg - 🔑 Speed of sound in air = 340 m/s (divides deflagration from detonation) - 🔑 Carbon steel weakening temperature ≈ 650 °C - 🔑 Conversion: 1 ppm × MW/24.45 = mg/m³ at 25°C, 1 atm > **End of Module 4 Notes — Fire, Explosion and Toxic Gas Release**

topics.title: Structure of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment, Identification of Hazards, Inventory Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT833 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structure of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment, Identification of Hazards, Inventory Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: STRUCTURE OF HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT (HIRA)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** is a systematic, structured, and proactive engineering methodology employed in process safety management to (a) recognize potential sources of harm (hazards), (b) characterize the credible accident scenarios arising from these hazards, and (c) evaluate the likelihood and severity of the resulting consequences in order to prioritize and implement risk control measures.

According to standard industrial safety frameworks (aligned with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, CCPS Guidelines, and ISO 12100), HIRA is the cornerstone element of a Process Safety Management (PSM) system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> A hazard is an **intrinsic property** of a material, system, or process that has the potential to cause harm to people, property, or the environment. **Risk** is the combination of the *probability* of an event occurring and the *magnitude* (severity) of its consequences.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Staircase of Safety"**
>
> Imagine you are inspecting a multi-story building under construction before it is occupied.
> - **Hazard Identification** is like walking through every floor with a checklist and pointing at each loose wire, slippery tile, or unguarded edge and saying, *"This thing here CAN hurt someone."*
> - **Risk Assessment** is the next step, where you ask two questions for each problem you found: *"How bad will the injury be?"* (severity) and *"How often might it happen?"* (probability).
> - **Inventory Analysis** is the preparatory step — before you walk the building, you first pull out the **blueprint/parts list** to know which materials are present (chemicals, machinery, energies) so you know *what* to look for.
>
> Without inventory analysis, you walk into the building blind. Without hazard identification, you don't know the problems exist. Without risk assessment, you don't know which problem to fix first. This three-stage ladder is the *Structure of HIRA*.

### 1.3 The Hierarchical Structure of HIRA

HIRA follows a top-down hierarchical structure commonly accepted in industrial process safety engineering:

1. **Stage 1 — Inventory Analysis** *(Foundation)*
   - Material inventory
   - Process/equipment inventory
   - Energy inventory
   - Chemical/physical property screening

2. **Stage 2 — Hazard Identification** *(Recognition Phase)*
   - Checklists
   - What-If Analysis
   - Hazard and Operability Studies (HAZOP)
   - Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
   - Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)
   - Event Tree Analysis (ETA)

3. **Stage 3 — Risk Estimation & Evaluation** *(Quantification Phase)*
   - Consequence analysis
   - Probability/frequency estimation
   - Risk = f(Severity × Probability)
   - Risk matrix evaluation
   - ALARP demonstration

4. **Stage 4 — Risk Control & Mitigation** *(Decision Phase)*
   - Inherent safety design
   - Engineering controls
   - Administrative controls
   - PPE
   - Emergency response planning

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Hazard**: A source of potential harm (intrinsic property).
- **Hazardous Event**: The immediate cause of an incident (e.g., release, rupture, ignition).
- **Top Event**: The undesired event at the top of a fault tree whose probability is being evaluated.
- **Initiating Event (IE)**: The first event in a sequence that starts an accident scenario.
- **Likelihood (L)**: Probability or frequency of occurrence, usually expressed as events per year.
- **Severity (S)**: Magnitude of consequence (fatality, injury, financial loss, environmental damage).
- **Risk (R)**: The mathematical product or qualitative combination of severity and likelihood.
- **Residual Risk**: The risk remaining after control measures have been applied.
- **ALARP**: As Low As Reasonably Practicable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Identification of Hazards — Taxonomy

Hazard identification is the **qualitative recognition** stage. It answers the question: *"What can go wrong?"* Hazards in industrial systems can be broadly classified into six canonical categories, which form the spine of every safety audit:

### 2.1.1 The Six Classes of Industrial Hazards

1. **Chemical Hazards**
   - Toxic substances (e.g., H₂S, Benzene, Phosgene)
   - Flammable substances (e.g., LPG, Acetone)
   - Reactive/oxidizing agents (e.g., Hydrogen peroxide, Nitrates)
   - Corrosives (e.g., HCl, NaOH)

2. **Physical Hazards**
   - Mechanical: Rotating shafts, gears, presses, conveyors
   - Electrical: High voltage, arc flash, static discharge
   - Thermal: Hot surfaces, molten metal, cryogenic fluids
   - Radiation: Ionizing (X-rays, gamma), Non-ionizing (UV, laser)

3. **Biological Hazards**
   - Bacteria, viruses, fungi (pharma, food, wastewater)

4. **Ergonomic Hazards**
   - Repetitive motion, awkward postures, manual handling

5. **Psychosocial Hazards**
   - Stress, fatigue, violence, harassment

6. **Environmental Hazards**
   - Noise, vibration, lighting, confined spaces

### 2.2 Inventory Analysis — The Foundational Pillar

**Inventory Analysis** is the systematic compilation and evaluation of all materials, equipment, processes, and energy sources present in a facility. It is the prerequisite for every hazard identification technique because *you cannot identify what you do not know exists*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Inventory Analysis establishes the **"What's in the plant?"** baseline before any hazard study can be meaningfully conducted. It is the data-gathering phase that feeds every subsequent HIRA tool (HAZOP, FMEA, QRA).

### 2.3 Components of a Comprehensive Inventory Analysis

The four key inventories that must be prepared are:

#### A. Material Inventory (Chemical Inventory)
A complete list of all raw materials, intermediates, products, by-products, and wastes present at the facility, with quantities and physical states. Includes:
- CAS (Chemical Abstracts Service) number
- Quantity (kg or tons stored/processed)
- Physical state (solid, liquid, gas)
- Storage conditions (pressure, temperature)

#### B. Process/Equipment Inventory
A list of all equipment items in the process, including:
- Reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers
- Pumps, compressors, piping
- Storage tanks, vessels
- Relief valves, instrumentation

#### C. Energy Inventory
A list of all forms of energy present in the system:
- Chemical energy (combustion)
- Mechanical energy (pressurized fluids, rotating equipment)
- Electrical energy (HV supply)
- Thermal energy (heat, cryogenics)
- Potential energy (elevated liquids, suspended loads)

#### D. Deviation/Credible Scenario Inventory
The list of "what-if" deviations generated during the hazard identification step.

### 2.4 Hazard Identification Techniques — Comparative Analysis

| Technique | Type | Application Stage | Focus | Output |
|-----------|------|-------------------|-------|--------|
| **Checklist** | Qualitative | Operating plant | Compliance with standards | Pass/Fail items |
| **What-If** | Qualitative | Conceptual/Detailed design | Brainstorming deviations | List of concerns |
| **HAZOP** | Qualitative | Detailed design through operation | Process deviations from design intent | Causes–Consequences–Safeguards |
| **FMEA** | Qualitative | Equipment level | Equipment failure modes | Risk Priority Number (RPN) |
| **FTA** | Quantitative | Complex systems | Logical combination of failures | Top event probability |
| **ETA** | Quantitative | Accident sequence modeling | Outcomes of initiating events | Outcome probabilities |
| **Bow-Tie** | Hybrid | Operational risk | Cause→Consequence linkage | Barrier-based risk picture |
| **QRA** | Quantitative | Major Hazard facilities | Numerical risk levels | Individual/Societal risk |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations constitute the **high-yield formula set** for Module 4 problems.

| Formula/Concept | Mathematical Form | Description | Units |
|-----------------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Risk (Fundamental)** | $R = f(S, P)$ | Risk as a function of severity and probability | — |
| **Risk Product Form** | $R = S \times P$ | Quantitative risk = severity × probability | (Damage × yr⁻¹) |
| **Risk Matrix Score** | $R_{score} = S_{score} \times L_{score}$ | Numerical rating for risk matrices | Dimensionless |
| **FMEA — RPN** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number | Dimensionless |
| **FTA — AND Gate** | $P_{TOP} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Probability through AND gate | yr⁻¹ |
| **FTA — OR Gate** | $P_{TOP} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)$ | Probability through OR gate | yr⁻¹ |
| **ETA — Outcome Frequency** | $f_{outcome} = f_{IE} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{branch,i}$ | Outcome branch frequency | yr⁻¹ |
| **Fatality Rate (FAR)** | $FAR = \frac{N_{fatalities} \times 10^8}{T_{person-hours}}$ | Fatal Accident Rate | per 10⁸ person-hr |
| **Individual Risk (IR)** | $IR = \sum_{i} f_i \times p_{f,i}$ | Probability of fatality at a location | yr⁻¹ |
| **F-N Curve** | $F(N) = \sum_i f_i \cdot \mathbb{1}[N_i \geq N]$ | Cumulative frequency of N+ fatalities | yr⁻¹ |

> **Units Convention**: $P$ = probability (yr⁻¹ for frequencies); $S$ = severity (often on 1–5 scale); $L$ = likelihood (often on 1–5 scale).

### 2.6 Why This Structure Matters (The "Why")

The **structured approach** is critical because:

1. **Completeness** — A systematic method ensures no hazard category is missed.
2. **Reproducibility** — Structured methods produce consistent results across different teams.
3. **Documentation** — Regulatory compliance (KTU, OSHA, EPA) requires auditable records.
4. **Communication** — A common framework (cause → consequence → safeguard) enables risk dialogue.
5. **Decision-making** — Quantitative outputs enable cost-benefit analysis of safety measures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Risk Matrix Numerical Scoring

The risk matrix is the most commonly used risk evaluation tool in KTU-level industrial safety assessments. The derivation of the numerical score proceeds from the basic definition of risk.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: State the foundational definition of risk.**

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Likelihood})$$

This is the universal starting point in industrial safety engineering.

**Step 2: Introduce the discrete ordinal scale used in qualitative matrices.**

Since severity and likelihood are usually rated on ordinal scales (1 to 4 or 1 to 5), the multiplicative product form is adopted:

$$R_{score} = S_{score} \times L_{score}$$

where $S_{score} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and $L_{score} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$.

**Step 3: Demonstrate boundary cases.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\min} &= 1 \times 1 = 1 \quad \text{(Negligible risk)} \\
R_{\max} &= 5 \times 5 = 25 \quad \text{(Catastrophic \& Frequent)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Categorize the resulting score into action bands.**

$$\begin{aligned}
1 \leq R \leq 4 &\rightarrow \text{Low Risk (Acceptable, monitor)} \\
5 \leq R \leq 12 &\rightarrow \text{Medium Risk (Mitigate with controls)} \\
13 \leq R \leq 25 &\rightarrow \text{High Risk (Immediate action required)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Example numerical evaluation.**

Consider a scenario: *A pump seal failure releasing flammable solvent.*

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{score} &= 4 \quad \text{(Major injury, environmental release)} \\
L_{score} &= 3 \quad \text{(Possible, has occurred in industry)} \\
R_{score} &= 4 \times 3 = 12 \quad \text{(Medium Risk band)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion logic**: Each ordinal rating corresponds to a calibrated consequence/probability band. The product gives a single scalar for prioritization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Top Event Probability

The Fault Tree uses Boolean logic gates to compute the probability of a Top Event from basic component failure probabilities.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define basic events with failure probabilities.**

Let $A$, $B$, $C$ be basic events with known failure probabilities $P_A$, $P_B$, $P_C$ (units: failures per year, i.e., yr⁻¹).

**Step 2: AND gate — Top event occurs only if ALL inputs occur.**

For two independent basic events joined by an AND gate:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{AND}} &= P(\text{A AND B}) \\
&= P(A) \cdot P(B) \quad \text{(independence assumption)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Generalized to $n$ events:

$$P_{\text{AND}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

**Step 3: OR gate — Top event occurs if ANY input occurs.**

For two independent events joined by an OR gate, use the complement rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{OR}} &= P(\text{A OR B}) \\
&= 1 - P(\text{NOT A AND NOT B}) \\
&= 1 - [1 - P(A)][1 - P(B)]
\end{aligned}$$

Generalized to $n$ events:

$$P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - P_i\right)$$

**Step 4: Numerical example — Reactor overpressure Top Event.**

Consider a Top Event *T* = "Reactor overpressure" with two causes in OR, each requiring both of two sub-events (AND).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cause 1: Cooling failure AND Operator error} \\
\text{Cause 2: Blocked vent AND Sensor failure}
\end{aligned}$$

Given: $P(\text{Cooling fail}) = 0.05\ \text{yr}^{-1}$, $P(\text{Operator err}) = 0.10\ \text{yr}^{-1}$, $P(\text{Vent block}) = 0.02\ \text{yr}^{-1}$, $P(\text{Sensor fail}) = 0.08\ \text{yr}^{-1}$.

**Step 5: Compute each AND-gate path.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{Cause 1}} &= 0.05 \times 0.10 = 0.005\ \text{yr}^{-1} \\
P_{\text{Cause 2}} &= 0.02 \times 0.08 = 0.0016\ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Compute OR gate (Top Event).**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_T &= 1 - (1 - 0.005)(1 - 0.0016) \\
&= 1 - (0.995)(0.9984) \\
&= 1 - 0.99341 \\
&= 0.00659\ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

This corresponds to a return period of approximately $1 / 0.00659 \approx 152$ years.

> **Conversion logic**: The independence assumption holds when component failures are not common-cause coupled. For dependent events, conditional probabilities must be used.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: FMEA — Risk Priority Number (RPN)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the three ordinal dimensions.**

- **Severity (S)**: 1 (negligible) to 5 (catastrophic)
- **Occurrence (O)**: 1 (very rare) to 5 (very frequent)
- **Detectability (D)**: 1 (almost certain detection) to 10 (almost impossible to detect) — *Note: KTU often simplifies to 1–5*

**Step 2: Compose the RPN.**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 3: Worked example — Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) Failure.**

Failure mode: *PSV fails to open on demand.*

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 5 \quad \text{(Catastrophic — vessel rupture possible)} \\
O &= 2 \quad \text{(Unlikely — yearly testing)} \\
D &= 3 \quad \text{(Moderate detection difficulty)} \\
RPN &= 5 \times 2 \times 3 = 30
\end{aligned}$$

A second failure mode of the same PSV:

Failure mode: *PSV opens spuriously (leakage).*

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 3 \quad \text{(Moderate — process upset)} \\
O &= 4 \quad \text{(Occasional)} \\
D &= 2 \quad \text{(Easy detection)} \\
RPN &= 3 \times 4 \times 2 = 24
\end{aligned}$$

The first failure mode has higher RPN and therefore higher priority for intervention.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Hazard Identification Technique

The selection of the appropriate HIRA technique depends on project phase, information availability, and the depth of analysis required. The following decision matrix is widely accepted:

| Project Phase | Information Available | Best Technique | Reason |
|---------------|----------------------|----------------|--------|
| Conceptual Design | Preliminary P&ID, MSDS | **What-If / Checklist** | Quick, low-data, broad sweep |
| Detailed Design | Full P&ID, control logic | **HAZOP** | Systematic node-by-node deviation analysis |
| Procurement / Construction | Vendor specs | **FMEA** | Equipment-level failure focus |
| Commissioning | Operating procedures | **What-If / HAZOP** | Operational deviations |
| Operating Plant | Maintenance records, incident history | **FMEA, FTA, Bow-Tie** | Refined understanding post-incident |
| Major Hazard Facility | All of above | **QRA (with FTA + ETA)** | Regulatory requirement, societal risk |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA). List any four hazard identification techniques used in process industries.**

**Model Answer:**

HIRA is a structured engineering methodology used in process safety management to systematically recognize hazards (sources of potential harm), characterize credible accident scenarios, and evaluate the resulting risk (combination of severity and probability) for the purpose of prioritizing risk reduction measures.

**Four hazard identification techniques:**
1. **Checklist Analysis** — Compliance review against predefined standards.
2. **What-If Analysis** — Brainstorming-based "what could go wrong" review.
3. **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** — Systematic deviation analysis using guide words.
4. **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** — Equipment-level failure analysis using RPN.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is Inventory Analysis? Why is it considered the foundation of HIRA?**

**Model Answer:**

Inventory Analysis is the systematic compilation and documentation of all materials, equipment, processes, and energy sources present in an industrial facility, including their quantities, locations, and hazardous properties.

It is the **foundation of HIRA** because:
- It establishes the "what is in the plant" baseline.
- Without a complete inventory, hazards cannot be systematically identified.
- It feeds accurate data (chemical properties, equipment specs) to all subsequent HIRA techniques (HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, QRA).
- It enables prioritization of high-risk materials/processes for detailed study.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment in detail. Describe the four stages with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reactor cooling system has two independent failure modes leading to overpressure (Top Event). Path 1 requires both Pump failure (P = 0.04 yr⁻¹) and Valve failure (P = 0.06 yr⁻¹). Path 2 requires both Sensor failure (P = 0.05 yr⁻¹) and Controller failure (P = 0.10 yr⁻¹). Calculate the top event frequency using FTA logic. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a)

The **Structure of HIRA** comprises four sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Inventory Analysis (Data Foundation)**
- **Material Inventory:** List all chemicals (e.g., 50 tons acetone, 200 kg chlorine cylinders) with CAS numbers and MSDS data.
- **Equipment Inventory:** Reactors (R-101), distillation columns (T-201), pumps (P-301A/B), storage tanks (TK-401).
- **Energy Inventory:** Steam (10 bar, 180°C), electricity (415 V, 3-phase), compressed air (7 bar).
- *Example:* In a chlor-alkali plant, inventory reveals 500 tons of liquid chlorine — flag for toxic release study.

**Stage 2 — Hazard Identification (Recognition Phase)**
Apply techniques such as HAZOP, What-If, FMEA. *Example:* HAZOP on reactor R-101 with guide word "NO" applied to "FLOW" reveals "no cooling water flow → exothermic runaway → pressure rise."

**Stage 3 — Risk Estimation (Quantification Phase)**
- Estimate consequence (e.g., 50 m toxic cloud radius).
- Estimate probability (e.g., 1×10⁻³ per year).
- Evaluate on risk matrix → *Medium Risk* category.

**Stage 4 — Risk Control (Mitigation Phase)**
- *Inherent:* Reduce chlorine inventory below threshold quantity.
- *Engineering:* Install redundant cooling water pump + auto-block valve.
- *Administrative:* Operator training, Permit-to-Work system.
- *PPE:* Full SCBA for emergency response team.

#### Solution to Part (b)

**Given data:**
- Path 1: Pump fail = 0.04 yr⁻¹, Valve fail = 0.06 yr⁻¹ (AND gate)
- Path 2: Sensor fail = 0.05 yr⁻¹, Controller fail = 0.10 yr⁻¹ (AND gate)
- Paths 1 and 2 joined by OR gate → Top Event.

**Step 1: Compute AND-gate probabilities for each path.**

$$P_{\text{Path 1}} = P_{\text{pump}} \times P_{\text{valve}} = 0.04 \times 0.06 = 0.0024\ \text{yr}^{-1}$$

$$P_{\text{Path 2}} = P_{\text{sensor}} \times P_{\text{controller}} = 0.05 \times 0.10 = 0.0050\ \text{yr}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Apply OR-gate formula for the Top Event.**

$$P_{\text{Top}} = 1 - (1 - P_{\text{Path 1}})(1 - P_{\text{Path 2}})$$

$$P_{\text{Top}} = 1 - (1 - 0.0024)(1 - 0.0050)$$

$$P_{\text{Top}} = 1 - (0.9976)(0.9950)$$

$$P_{\text{Top}} = 1 - 0.99262$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{Top}} = 0.00738\ \text{yr}^{-1}}$$

This corresponds to a return period of $1 / 0.00738 \approx 135.5$ years, which exceeds typical corporate risk tolerance of 10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹ and demands immediate risk reduction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain Inventory Analysis in detail. Describe the four major inventories that must be prepared with appropriate examples for a petroleum refinery. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An FMEA study on a centrifugal pump identifies the following failure mode: *"Mechanical seal leakage."* Using Severity (S) = 4, Occurrence (O) = 3, and Detection difficulty (D) = 4, compute the RPN. If the company target is RPN < 40, recommend two engineering controls. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a)

**Inventory Analysis** is the systematic process of compiling all relevant information about materials, equipment, processes, and energy sources within a facility. For a **petroleum refinery**, the four inventories are:

**1. Material Inventory (Chemical Inventory)**

Lists all hydrocarbons and chemicals with quantities and storage conditions. Example for a typical refinery:

| Material | Quantity | Storage Condition | Hazard |
|----------|----------|-------------------|--------|
| Crude Oil | 50,000 tons | Floating roof tank, atmospheric | Flammable liquid |
| Naphtha | 5,000 m³ | Cone roof tank | Highly flammable |
| LPG (Propane/Butane) | 2,000 tons | Pressure spheres (10 bar) | Flammable gas under pressure |
| H₂S (sour gas) | ppm level | Process streams | Highly toxic |
| Sulfuric Acid (alkylation) | 200 tons | Tank, ambient | Corrosive |

**2. Process/Equipment Inventory**

Lists all process units and equipment: Atmospheric & vacuum distillation columns, Fluidized Catalytic Cracker (FCC), Hydrocracker, Catalytic Reformer, Alkylation unit, Sulfur recovery unit, Pumps, Compressors, Heat exchangers, Air-fin coolers, Storage spheres.

**3. Energy Inventory**

- **Chemical Energy:** Hydrocarbon fuel inventory (millions of MJ latent combustion energy).
- **Thermal Energy:** Fired heaters (furnace outlet 400°C), steam system (HP/MP/LP).
- **Mechanical Energy:** Compressors at 50 bar discharge, pumps at 30 bar.
- **Electrical Energy:** 11 kV / 415 V distribution.

**4. Deviation/Credible Scenario Inventory**

Derived from HAZOP — e.g., "loss of cooling to hydrocracker reactor," "H₂S release from amine regenerator," "LPG sphere overpressure."

#### Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1: Compute the RPN.**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

$$RPN = 4 \times 3 \times 4$$

$$\boxed{RPN = 48}$$

**Step 2: Compare to company target.**

Since $48 > 40$, the failure mode **fails** the target and requires corrective action.

**Step 3: Recommend two engineering controls.**

1. **Seal Pot / Barrier Fluid System Upgrade:** Install a pressurized barrier fluid system (Plan 53B) that provides a clean, pressurized buffer between the process fluid and the seal faces. This prevents dry running and contamination, dramatically reducing seal leakage events.

2. **Vibration & Temperature Monitoring with Trip:** Install continuous wireless vibration sensors and seal-flush temperature probes. Connect signals to DCS with alarm at threshold and auto-pump-trip at critical level to interrupt seal degradation before leakage occurs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students often write RPN = 12 by adding instead of multiplying ($S+O+D$). **Always multiply.** Also, ensure each rating scale (S, O, D) is referenced from the *same* calibrated scoring matrix used by the company — using mixed scales (1–5 for S, 1–10 for D) is a common error in board exams.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in HIRA Questions (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Hazard with Risk:** A *hazard* is a property; *risk* involves probability. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these terms are interchanged.
> 2. **Omitting Inventory Analysis:** Many students jump directly to HAZOP without describing the inventory. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly awards 2 marks for the inventory foundation.
> 3. **Independent Event Assumption:** In FTA problems, students forget to state that component failures are assumed *independent* before applying the AND-gate multiplication.
> 4. **Units in OR Gate:** Writing $P_{OR} = P_1 + P_2 + \dots$ is **wrong** (only valid for small probabilities). Always use the complement formula.
> 5. **Skipping ALARP:** After risk evaluation, students must conclude with a control plan referencing ALARP — omitting this loses a full mark.
> 6. **Mixing FMEA scales:** Using S scale 1–5 with D scale 1–10 without justification is flagged.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4**
>
> - [x] **HIRA** = Hazard Identification + Risk Assessment; the *core* of Process Safety Management.
> - [x] **Hazard** is intrinsic; **Risk** involves probability × severity.
> - [x] **Structure of HIRA** = 4 stages: Inventory → Identification → Estimation → Control.
> - [x] **Inventory Analysis** is the foundation; it produces Material, Equipment, Energy, and Scenario inventories.
> - [x] **Identification Techniques:** Checklist, What-If, HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, ETA, Bow-Tie, QRA.
> - [x] **HAZOP** uses guide words: NO/NOT, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN.
> - [x] **FMEA RPN** = $S \times O \times D$. Higher RPN → higher priority for action.
> - [x] **FTA AND gate** = product of child probabilities; **OR gate** = 1 − product of complements.
> - [x] **ETA** traces *forward* from an initiating event through success/failure branches to outcomes.
> - [x] **Risk Matrix** typical scales 1–5; score = $S \times L$; bands: Low (1–4), Medium (5–12), High (13–25).
> - [x] **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable; residual risk must be ALARP before acceptance.
> - [x] **Inherent Safety** hierarchy: Minimize → Substitute → Moderate → Simplify.
> - [x] Always state the **independence assumption** in FTA calculations.
> - [x] Always report the **return period** (1/frequency) in years when computing frequencies.
> - [x] Six hazard classes: Chemical, Physical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial, Environmental.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT833 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Plant Hazard Rating** is a systematic, quantitative engineering methodology used to evaluate the relative magnitude of fire, explosion, and reactive chemical hazards in a chemical/petrochemical process unit. It translates qualitative process knowledge into a numerical score that quantifies the **loss-of-containment potential** and the probable magnitude of damage to property, people, and business continuity.

The discipline is governed by internationally accepted indices:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Hazard Rating Systems Studied in KTU OEAUT833:**
> 1. **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)** – American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE) / Dow Chemical Company method, applicable to 5,000+ processes.
> 2. **Mond Index (Dow Chemical Explosions Index)** – Refinement of the Dow Index for chemical plants using oxygen, chlorinated hydrocarbons, or having complex inventories.
> 3. **Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)** – AIChE method used to rank acute toxic hazards to the surrounding public.
> 4. **IFAL (Instantaneous Fractional Annual Loss)** – A risk-ranking companion to the Dow F&EI used to prioritize loss prevention actions.

The **Dow Fire and Explosion Index** is the most commonly examined topic in KTU 2024 Scheme assessments. It gives a single number (the F&EI score) that classifies a process unit as **Light, Moderate, Heavy, or Severe**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome for this module is **CO4 – "Apply hazard evaluation and risk analysis techniques to industrial systems"** (Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze). Students must therefore be able to *calculate* a Dow F&EI score and *interpret* its consequence implications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a process plant as a **kitchen**. The stovetop (the reactor), the gas cylinder (raw material storage), and the chimney (vent stack) each have different "danger scores." A **hazard rating system** is like a **food-safety hygiene rating (0–5 stars)** posted at the restaurant window — it gives the customer (the regulator / insurer / public) a quick, single number to judge the *relative* safety of a complex kitchen.

In a chemical plant:
- The **stove** = a pressurized reactor holding flammable chemicals → high score.
- The **pantry** = an atmospheric storage tank with low vapor pressure → moderate score.
- The **sink** = a water-treatment basin with non-flammable chemicals → low score.

The Dow F&EI acts as this **hygiene rating for a process unit**, summing up (a) how nasty the material is (Material Factor) and (b) how it is being used (Process Hazards).

## 1.3 Underlying Physical/Chemical Constants

The most important constants used in hazard rating are:

- **NFPA 704 Hazard Diamond Ratings** (Health, Flammability, Reactivity), each rated **0 to 4**.
- **Material Factor (MF)** – tabulated dimensionless number (typically **1 to 40**).
- **Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c)** of hydrocarbons ≈ **40 to 50 MJ/kg** (≈ 11.5 to 14 kWh/kg).
- **Heat of Decomposition (ΔH_d)** – energy released by exothermic decomposition of unstable molecules.
- **Standard DOW Categories**: Light (F&EI = 1–60), Moderate (61–96), Heavy (97–127), Severe (≥ 128).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Value:** A process unit with F&EI ≥ 128 is considered **Severe** and demands immediate loss-prevention engineering review and active protection systems (fire suppression, blast walls, gas detection).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) — Architecture

The Dow F&EI is built as a **bottom-up penalty multiplier** model. The final F&EI score is the product of how hazardous the *material* is, multiplied by how badly the *process* is operated.

### Step 1 — Material Factor (MF)

The Material Factor quantifies the intrinsic energy-release potential of the chemical handled.

- Determined from **NFPA 704 ratings** (H, F, R) and the **Heat of Combustion / Decomposition**.
- If the chemical is **combustible**: lookup table based on NFPA Flammability (F) rating.
- If the chemical is **combustible AND unstable / decomposable**: MF includes an additional penalty using NFPA Reactivity (R).
- Dust-handling processes use a special dust-MF table.

> **Reference MF values (Flammability rating → MF):**
>
> | NFPA F Rating | NFPA R Rating | Material Factor (MF) |
> |:-------------:|:-------------:|:--------------------:|
> | 0 | 0 | 1 to 4 (non-flammable) |
> | 1 | 0 | 4 to 10 |
> | 2 | 0 | 10 to 14 |
> | 3 | 0 | 14 to 18 |
> | 4 | 0 | 21 to 24 |
> | 1–4 | 1 | +1 to +4 adjustment |
> | 1–4 | 2 | +2 to +8 adjustment |
> | 1–4 | 3 | +3 to +12 adjustment |
> | 1–4 | 4 | +4 to +16 adjustment |

### Step 2 — General Process Hazards (GPH)

Six penalty categories describing the *general* configuration of the process:

1. **Exothermic chemical reactions** (penalty 0.30 to 1.25).
2. **Endothermic reactions** (penalty 0.20 to 0.40).
3. **Material handling and transfer** (0.10 to 1.05).
4. **Enclosed or indoor process units** (0.25 to 0.90).
5. **Access** (0.20 to 0.35).
6. **Drainage and spill control** (0.10 to 0.50).

$$\text{GPH} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} \text{Penalty}_i$$

### Step 3 — Special Process Hazards (SPH)

Twelve penalty categories describing *specific* conditions that increase frequency/severity:

1. **Toxic materials** (0.20 to 0.80).
2. **Sub-atmospheric pressure** (0.50).
3. **Operation in or near flammable range**:
   - **Tankage** of flammable liquids (0.50 to 1.00).
   - **Unstable liquids** (penalty 0.60 to 2.00).
   - **Boiling / diffused liquids** (0.30 to 1.75).
4. **Dust or mist explosion** (0.25 to 2.00).
5. **Pressure** above atmospheric (penalty function of psig).
6. **Low temperature** (0.20 to 0.30).
7. **Quantity of material at risk** (penalty by inventory mass).
8. **Corrosion and erosion** (0.10 to 0.75).
9. **Leakage from joints and packing** (0.10 to 1.50).
10. **Use of fired heaters** (0.10 to 1.00).
11. **Hot oil heat exchange system** (0.15 to 1.15).
12. **Rotating equipment** (0.50).

$$\text{SPH} = \sum_{j=1}^{12} \text{Penalty}_j$$

### Step 4 — Process Unit Hazards Factor (F3)

$$\boxed{F_3 = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH}}$$

### Step 5 — Dow Fire and Explosion Index

$$\boxed{\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The F&EI is a multiplier, not a probability.** It is used to *rank* process units against each other and to compute a damage radius, NOT to predict the absolute likelihood of a fire.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols | Units |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:--------|:------|
| 1 | Material Factor (Lookup) | MF = f(NFPA H, F, R) | — | dimensionless |
| 2 | General Process Hazards | $\text{GPH} = \sum \text{Penalties}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 3 | Special Process Hazards | $\text{SPH} = \sum \text{Penalties}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 4 | Process Unit Hazards Factor | $F_3 = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH}$ | GPH, SPH | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Dow Fire & Explosion Index** | $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3$ | MF, F3 | dimensionless |
| 6 | Hazard Index | $\text{HI} = \text{F\&EI}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 7 | Damage Factor (lookup) | $\text{DF} = f(\text{F\&EI})$ | — | dimensionless (0.00 to 1.00) |
| 8 | Base MPPD | $\text{Base MPPD} = \text{DF} \times \text{Replacement Value}$ | — | ₹ / $ |
| 9 | Actual MPPD | $\text{MPPDo} = \text{Base MPPD} \times \text{Loss Control Credit Factor}$ | — | ₹ / $ |
| 10 | Loss Control Credit Factor | $\text{LCCF} = (1 - C_1)(1 - C_2)\cdots(1 - C_7)$ | $C_i$ (Credit Fraction) | dimensionless |
| 11 | Mond Fire Damage Index | $D = B \times \dfrac{1 + M}{100} \times \dfrac{1 + P}{100} \times \dfrac{1 + S}{100} \times \dfrac{1 + T}{100} \times \dfrac{1 + U}{100} \times \dfrac{1 + L}{100}$ | B = Unit Hazard Base, M = Moisture, P = Particle, S = Static, T = Thermal, U = Translation, L = Layout | dimensionless |
| 12 | Mond Explosion Damage Index | $E = D \times (1 + m/100)$ | m = mechanical energy correction | dimensionless |
| 13 | CEI | $\text{CEI} = (6551 \times 10^{\log V / 5}) \div (P^{0.403})$ | V = vapor pressure, P = ERPG-2 limit (ppm) | dimensionless |
| 14 | MP-DO (Maximum Probable Days Outage) | $\text{MP-DO} = f(\text{MPPDo}, \text{Annual Sales})$ | — | days |

> **Notes on the table:**
> - MPPD = **Maximum Probable Property Damage** (the cost of replacement and repair after a single major incident).
> - MPPDo = **Maximum Probable Property Damage Online** (after active + passive loss-control credits).
> - LCCF is the *product* of (1 − Credit) for each of 7 loss-prevention categories: Process Control (C1), Material Isolation (C2), Fire Protection (C3), …, Emergency Response (C7).

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Plant Design Phase**: Engineers use F&EI to choose safer process routes (intrinsically safer design).
- **Insurance Underwriting**: Insurance companies use F&EI scores to set **fire insurance premiums** (e.g., ICICI Lombard, Tata AIG).
- **Regulatory Compliance**: OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119) and Indian Factories Act (Amended 1987) require hazard analysis for threshold quantities.
- **Plant Layout (API RP 752/753)**: Damage radius (R) computed from F&EI dictates separation distances between units.
- **Risk Prioritization**: Helps allocate limited capital between competing process units for retrofit investments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — A Hydrocarbon Storage Unit (KTU-Type Problem)

> **Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark):**
> A process plant stores 200,000 kg of toluene in an atmospheric tank. The unit has the following penalties:
>
> - Exothermic reaction: **0.30** (mild)
> - Material transfer (pumped): **0.50**
> - Outdoor unit, no access penalty: **0.20**
> - Drainage: **0.20**
> - Toxic material (toluene vapour): **0.40**
> - Tankage of flammable liquid: **0.50**
> - Pressure (atmospheric, no penalty): **0.00**
> - Quantity stored > 100,000 kg: **0.60**
> - Corrosion: **0.30**
> - Rotating equipment (pumps): **0.50**
> - **Capital replacement value** of the process unit: **₹ 50 crore**.
>
> **Compute:** (a) the Dow F&EI score, (b) the Damage Factor, and (c) the Base Maximum Probable Property Damage (Base MPPD).

### Step 1 — Material Factor of Toluene

Toluene is a flammable liquid, NFPA 704 ratings: H = 2, **F = 3, R = 0**. From the Dow MF table, a flammability rating of 3 with no reactivity penalty gives:

$$\text{MF}_{\text{toluene}} = 16$$

### Step 2 — Compute General Process Hazards (GPH)

Summing the four contributing GPH penalties given:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{GPH} &= 0.30 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.20 \\
&= 1.20
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Special Process Hazards (SPH)

Summing the five SPH penalties provided:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SPH} &= 0.40 + 0.50 + 0.00 + 0.60 + 0.30 + 0.50 \\
&= 2.30
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Process Unit Hazards Factor (F3)

$$\begin{aligned}
F_3 &= \text{GPH} + \text{SPH} \\
&= 1.20 + 2.30 \\
&= 3.50
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Dow Fire and Explosion Index

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{F\&EI} &= \text{MF} \times F_3 \\
&= 16 \times 3.50 \\
&= 56.00
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the F&EI

Referring to the DOW hazard table:

| F&EI Range | Hazard Degree |
|:----------:|:--------------:|
| 1 – 60 | **Light** |
| 61 – 96 | Moderate |
| 97 – 127 | Heavy |
| ≥ 128 | Severe |

$$\text{F\&EI} = 56 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Hazard Degree} = \textbf{Light}$$

### Step 7 — Damage Factor (DF) Lookup

For F&EI = 56, the corresponding **Damage Factor** is read from the DOW DF curve as:

$$\text{DF} = 0.32$$

### Step 8 — Base Maximum Probable Property Damage (Base MPPD)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Base MPPD} &= \text{DF} \times \text{Replacement Value} \\
&= 0.32 \times \text{₹ } 50,00,00,000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 16,00,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

That is, the **Base MPPD = ₹ 16 crore** before any loss-control credits are applied.

### Step 9 — Actual MPPD (after Loss Control Credits)

If the plant has installed the following credits:
- C1 (Process Control) = 0.20
- C2 (Material Isolation) = 0.20
- C3 (Fire Protection) = 0.10
- C4 (Explosion Protection) = 0.10
- C5 (Equipment Design) = 0.05
- C6 (Plant Layout) = 0.05
- C7 (Emergency Response) = 0.10

The Loss Control Credit Factor is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LCCF} &= (1 - C_1)(1 - C_2)(1 - C_3)(1 - C_4)(1 - C_5)(1 - C_6)(1 - C_7) \\
&= 0.80 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 \\
&= 0.80 \times 0.80 = 0.640 \\
&\quad \times 0.90 = 0.576 \\
&\quad \times 0.90 = 0.5184 \\
&\quad \times 0.95 = 0.4925 \\
&\quad \times 0.95 = 0.4679 \\
&\quad \times 0.90 = 0.4211
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore the **Actual MPPD** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MPPDo} &= \text{Base MPPD} \times \text{LCCF} \\
&= 16.00 \text{ Cr} \times 0.4211 \\
&= \text{₹ } 6.74 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}$$

The protection systems have reduced the **Maximum Probable Property Damage** from ₹ 16 Cr to **₹ 6.74 Cr** — a 57.9 % reduction in expected loss.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mond Index vs Dow F&EI

| Feature | Dow F&EI (1976) | Mond Index (1979) |
|:--------|:----------------|:------------------|
| Originator | Dow Chemical / AIChE | ICI (Imperial Chemical Industries) |
| Number of penalty terms | 18 (6 GPH + 12 SPH) | 35+ |
| Considers hydrogen, oxygen, chlorinated HC | Partial | **Yes (special features)** |
| Considers toxicity | Indirect (via SPH) | **Direct (TOX factor)** |
| Considers layout | Indirect | **Yes (L factor)** |
| Considers housekeeping | No | **Yes (G factor)** |
| Output | Single F&EI number | D, E, U, R, V, A sub-indices |
| Computational complexity | Low–Medium | High |
| **KTU weightage** | **★★★★★** | **★★★** |

> **Key Insight (Mond Index):** The Mond index introduces the concept of a **Toxicity Sub-Index (TOX)**, which Dow F&EI lacks. It also defines the **Overall Risk Rating (R) = D × U × V** which is used to prioritize plant-level risk reduction.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Dow F&EI Calculation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Select Process Unit"] --> B["Identify Chemicals Handled"]
    B --> C["Obtain NFPA 704 Ratings H F R"]
    C --> D{"Material Flammable or Combustible?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Lookup Material Factor MF"]
    D -- "No" --> F["MF = 1 to 4 only"]
    E --> G["Evaluate 6 General Process Hazards"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Sum Penalties to Obtain GPH"]
    H --> I["Evaluate 12 Special Process Hazards"]
    I --> J["Sum Penalties to Obtain SPH"]
    J --> K["Compute Process Unit Hazards Factor F3 = GPH + SPH"]
    K --> L["Compute Dow F&E Index F&EI = MF x F3"]
    L --> M{"Interpret F&EI Range"}
    M -- "1 to 60" --> N["Light Hazard"]
    M -- "61 to 96" --> O["Moderate Hazard"]
    M -- "97 to 127" --> P["Heavy Hazard"]
    M -- "128 and above" --> Q["Severe Hazard"]
    N --> R["Lookup Damage Factor DF"]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S["Compute Base MPPD = DF x Replacement Value"]
    S --> T["Apply Loss Control Credits C1 to C7"]
    T --> U["Compute LCCF = Product of 1 minus Ci"]
    U --> V["Compute Actual MPPD = Base MPPD x LCCF"]
    V --> W["Compute Business Interruption MP DO"]
    W --> X["End: Final Risk Ranking Report"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Mond Index Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Material Factor B"] --> B["Inventory Factor M"]
    B --> C["Penalty Block P S T U L"]
    C --> D["Fire Damage Index D"]
    A2["Special Features Y F M T Q H"] --> E["Explosion Damage Index E = D x 1 plus m"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Overall Risk Rating R = D x U x V"]
    F --> G["Hazard Classification: Improving Moderate Critical Catastrophic"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Loss Control Credit Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LossControlLayer["Loss Control Credit Categories C1 to C7"]
        C1["C1: Process Control Systems"]
        C2["C2: Material Isolation"]
        C3["C3: Fire Protection"]
        C4["C4: Explosion Protection"]
        C5["C5: Equipment Design"]
        C6["C6: Plant Layout"]
        C7["C7: Emergency Response"]
    end
    BaseMPPD["Base MPPD Input"] --> MultiplyNode["LCCF = Product of 1 minus Ci"]
    C1 --> MultiplyNode
    C2 --> MultiplyNode
    C3 --> MultiplyNode
    C4 --> MultiplyNode
    C5 --> MultiplyNode
    C6 --> MultiplyNode
    C7 --> MultiplyNode
    MultiplyNode --> ActualMPPD["Actual MPPD Output"]
    ActualMPPD --> RiskReport["Final Risk Assessment Report"]
```
