# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Dow F&EI Calculation Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Process Unit] --> B[Step 1: Determine Material Factor MF from Table]
    B --> C[Step 2: Evaluate General Process Hazards GPH]
    C --> D[Step 3: Evaluate Special Process Hazards SPH]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute F3 equals MF multiplied by GPH]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute FandEI equals F3 multiplied by SPH]
    F --> G[Step 6: Look up Damage Factor DF from Monograph]
    G --> H[Step 7: Compute Radius of Exposure R equals 0.84 times FandEI meters]
    H --> I[Step 8: Compute Area of Exposure A equals pi times R squared]
    I --> J[Step 9: Compute Base MPPD equals A times Veq times DF]
    J --> K[Step 10: Read Base MPDO from Monograph Figure 4]
    K --> L[Step 11: Compute Base BI equals MPPD divided by 30 times MPDO times 0.70]
    L --> M[Step 12: Apply Loss Control Credit Factors C1 C2 C3]
    M --> N[Step 13: Compute Credit Factor C equals C1 times C2 times C3]
    N --> O[Step 14: Compute Actual MPPD equals MPPDbase times C]
    O --> P[Step 15: Compute Actual MPDO equals MPDObase times C]
    P --> Q[Step 16: Compute Actual BI equals MPPDactual divided by 30 times MPDOactual times 0.70]
    Q --> R[End: Final Loss Estimation Report]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Hierarchical Factor Composition

```mermaid
graph LR
    MF[Material Factor MF]:::base --> F3
    GPH[General Process Hazards GPH]:::base --> F3
    F3[Process Unit Hazard Factor F3]:::intermediate --> FEI
    SPH[Special Process Hazards SPH]:::base --> FEI
    FEI[Fire and Explosion Index FandEI]:::final --> R[Radius of Exposure]
    FEI --> DF[Damage Factor DF from Monograph]
    R --> A[Area of Exposure]
    DF --> MPPD[Base MPPD]
    A --> MPPD
    Veq[Replacement Value Veq] --> MPPD
    MPPD --> BI[Base Business Interruption]
    C[Loss Control Credit C1 C2 C3] --> MPPDA[Actual MPPD]
    C --> MPDA[Actual MPDO]
    MPPDA --> BIA[Actual BI]

    classDef base fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef intermediate fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#78350f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef final fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — F&EI Hazard Band Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compute FandEI] --> Check1{FandEI between 1 and 60}
    Check1 -- YES --> Light[Light Hazard: Routine Precautions]
    Check1 -- NO --> Check2{FandEI between 61 and 96}
    Check2 -- YES --> Moderate[Moderate Hazard: Standard Fire Protection]
    Check2 -- NO --> Check3{FandEI between 97 and 127}
    Check3 -- YES --> Intermediate[Intermediate Hazard: Enhanced Protection]
    Check3 -- NO --> Check4{FandEI between 128 and 158}
    Check4 -- YES --> Heavy[Heavy Hazard: Major Engineering Review]
    Check4 -- NO --> Severe[Severe Hazard: Mandatory Redesign or Relocation]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Dow Index System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        I1[Material Properties: Flash Point, Heat of Combustion, Reactivity]
        I2[Process Data: Temperature, Pressure, Quantity]
        I3[Layout Data: Indoor or Outdoor, Drainage, Access]
    end

    subgraph FactorLayer[Factor Computation Layer]
        F1[Material Factor Lookup Table]
        F2[GPH Penalty Calculator]
        F3[SPH Penalty Calculator]
    end

    subgraph IndexLayer[Index Computation Layer]
        IX1[F3 equals MF times GPH]
        IX2[FandEI equals F3 times SPH]
        IX3[Damage Factor Lookup]
    end

    subgraph GeometryLayer[Geometry and Loss Layer]
        G1[Radius of Exposure R]
        G2[Area of Exposure A]
        L1[MPPD base]
        L2[MPDO base from Chart]
        L3[BI base]
    end

    subgraph CreditLayer[Loss Control Credit Layer]
        CL1[Process Control C1]
        CL2[Material Isolation C2]
        CL3[Fire Protection C3]
        CL4[Combined Credit C]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        O1[Actual MPPD]
        O2[Actual MPDO]
        O3[Actual Business Interruption]
        O4[Final Risk Report and Mitigation Plan]
    end

    I1 --> F1
    I2 --> F2
    I2 --> F3
    I3 --> F2
    F1 --> IX1
    F2 --> IX1
    IX1 --> IX2
    F3 --> IX2
    IX2 --> IX3
    IX2 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    IX3 --> L1
    G2 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L1 --> L3
    L2 --> L3
    CL1 --> CL4
    CL2 --> CL4
    CL3 --> CL4
    L1 --> O1
    L2 --> O2
    L3 --> O3
    CL4 --> O1
    CL4 --> O2
    O1 --> O4
    O2 --> O4
    O3 --> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Dow F&EI methodology is essentially a **5-layer functional architecture**: Input → Factor Computation → Index Computation → Geometry/Loss → Credit Adjustment → Output. The arrows show the **unidirectional data flow** — there is no feedback loop in the standard methodology.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Material Factor" as used in the Dow Fire and Explosion Index method. How is it determined for a given substance?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Material Factor (MF)** is a numerical measure of the **intrinsic energy release potential** of the most hazardous substance present in a process unit. It is the foundational parameter of the Dow F&EI methodology and represents the **inherent fire and explosion potential** of the material, independent of process conditions.

It is determined by:
1. **Identifying the most hazardous substance** in the unit (highest MF value).
2. **Reading the MF value** directly from **Table 2 of the Dow Chemical Company 7th Edition F&EI Monograph**. The table is indexed by substance name and is based on the **Heat of Combustion (ΔHc)** and the **Reactivity** of the chemical.

For example, **acetone has MF = 16**, **propane has MF = 21**, and **hydrogen has MF = 29**. The MF cannot be calculated; it is a tabulated, experimentally validated value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the substance is not in the table, MF is estimated from the **NFPA 704 reactivity rating** and the **heat of combustion** using the Dow correlation chart (Appendix B of the 7th Edition).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between General Process Hazards (GPH) and Special Process Hazards (SPH) in the Dow F&EI method. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | General Process Hazards (GPH) | Special Process Hazards (SPH) |
|---------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Nature | Generic, layout-related, or environmental risks | Process-specific, operational risks |
| Number of categories | 6 (in 7th Ed.) | 12 (in 7th Ed.) |
| Range of penalty | 0.20 – 1.25 per category | 0.10 – 3.00 per category |
| Typical baseline | 1.0 (no penalty) | 1.0 (no penalty) |
| Effect | Affects Process Unit Hazard Factor F₃ | Affects Fire & Explosion Index F&EI directly |
| Examples | 1. Enclosed/indoor process unit 2. Exothermic chemical reaction | 1. Quantity of flammable material 2. Leakage from joints and packing |

**Two examples each:**

- **GPH examples:**
  1. **Exothermic chemical reaction** (penalty 0.30 – 1.25) — e.g., a sulfonation reactor.
  2. **Material handling and transfer** (penalty 0.25 – 1.05) — e.g., pumping of a flammable solvent.

- **SPH examples:**
  1. **Quantity of flammable material** (penalty up to 3.00) — e.g., 10,000 kg of acetone.
  2. **Relief pressure setting** (penalty 0.16 – 1.40) — e.g., a relief valve set at 10 bar.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

> **A process unit in a chemical plant stores 6,000 kg of methanol at 2 bar gauge pressure and 30 °C. The following penalties apply:**
>
> - **GPH penalties:** Exothermic reaction = 0.30; Enclosed process unit = 0.50; Material handling = 0.40.
> - **SPH penalties:** Operation in flammable range = 0.50; Relief pressure = 0.20; Quantity of flammable = 0.85; Leakage from joints = 0.30; Corrosion and erosion = 0.20.
> - **Material Factor (methanol) = 16**
> - **Damage Factor (DF) = 0.50** (as read from the Dow chart)
> - **Replacement value of equipment in the unit = ₹ 4,00,00,000**
> - **Loss control credits:** C₁ = 0.93, C₂ = 0.95, C₃ = 0.98
> - **MPDO base = 24 days** (read from Dow Figure 4)
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** Material Factor, GPH, SPH, F₃, and the Fire & Explosion Index. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Radius of Exposure, Area of Exposure, Base MPPD, and Actual Business Interruption. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — F&EI Computation**

**Step 1: Material Factor (MF)**

$$
MF = 16 \quad \text{(from Dow 7th Ed. Table 2 for methanol)}
$$

**Step 2: General Process Hazards (GPH)**

$$
GPH = 1.0 + 0.30 + 0.50 + 0.40
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
GPH = 1.0 + 0.30 + 0.50 + 0.40 = 2.20
$$

$$
\boxed{GPH = 2.20}
$$

**Step 3: Special Process Hazards (SPH)**

$$
SPH = 1.0 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.85 + 0.30 + 0.20
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
SPH = 1.0 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.85 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 3.05
$$

$$
\boxed{SPH = 3.05}
$$

**Step 4: Process Unit Hazard Factor (F₃)**

$$
F_3 = MF \times GPH = 16 \times 2.20 = 35.2
$$

$$
\boxed{F_3 = 35.2}
$$

**Step 5: Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI)**

$$
F\&EI = F_3 \times SPH = 35.2 \times 3.05
$$

Computing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
35.2 \times 3.05 &= 35.2 \times (3 + 0.05) \\
&= 35.2 \times 3 + 35.2 \times 0.05 \\
&= 105.6 + 1.76 \\
&= 107.36
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F\&EI = 107.36 \approx 107}
$$

> **Hazard band:** F&EI = 107 → **Intermediate** (97 – 127 range).

---

**Part (b) — Loss Estimation**

**Step 6: Radius of Exposure (R)**

$$
R = 0.84 \times F\&EI = 0.84 \times 107.36
$$

Computing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.84 \times 107.36 &= 0.84 \times 107 + 0.84 \times 0.36 \\
&= 89.88 + 0.3024 \\
&= 90.18
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 90.18 \ \text{meters}}
$$

**Step 7: Area of Exposure (A)**

$$
A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (90.18)^2
$$

First compute $R^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(90.18)^2 &= (90 + 0.18)^2 \\
&= 8100 + 2 \times 90 \times 0.18 + 0.0324 \\
&= 8100 + 32.4 + 0.0324 \\
&= 8132.4324
\end{aligned}
$$

Now:

$$
A = 3.1416 \times 8132.4324 \approx 25{,}549.9 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 25{,}550 \ \text{m}^2}
$$

**Step 8: Base MPPD**

For this problem we use the given replacement value directly:

$$
MPPD_{base} = A \times \frac{V_{eq,\,total}}{A} \times DF = V_{eq,\,total} \times DF
$$

(Alternative direct formula if the value given is the total in area: $MPPD = V_{eq} \times DF$)

Assuming $V_{eq}$ is the total value within the area of exposure:

$$
MPPD_{base} = 4{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{MPPD_{base} = \text{₹} \ 2.00 \ \text{Crore}}
$$

**Step 9: Base BI**

$$
BI_{base} = \frac{MPPD_{base}}{30} \times MPDO_{base} \times 0.70
$$

Substituting:

$$
BI_{base} = \frac{2{,}00{,}00{,}000}{30} \times 24 \times 0.70
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2{,}00{,}00{,}000}{30} &= 6{,}66{,}666.67 \\[4pt]
6{,}66{,}666.67 \times 24 &= 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 &= 1{,}12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{BI_{base} = \text{₹} \ 1.12 \ \text{Crore}}
$$

**Step 10: Credit Factor (C)**

$$
C = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3 = 0.93 \times 0.95 \times 0.98
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.93 \times 0.95 &= 0.8835 \\
0.8835 \times 0.98 &= 0.86583
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 0.866}
$$

**Step 11: Actual MPPD, MPDO, BI**

$$
MPPD_{actual} = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.866 = 1{,}73{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
MPDO_{actual} = 24 \times 0.866 = 20.78 \ \text{days}
$$

$$
BI_{actual} = \frac{1{,}73{,}20{,}000}{30} \times 20.78 \times 0.70
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1{,}73{,}20{,}000}{30} &= 5{,}77{,}333.33 \\[4pt]
5{,}77{,}333.33 \times 20.78 &= 1{,}19{,}98{,}000 \\[4pt]
1{,}19{,}98{,}000 \times 0.70 &= 83{,}98{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{BI_{actual} = \text{₹} \ 83.99 \ \text{Lakhs} \approx \text{₹} \ 0.84 \ \text{Crore}}
$$

---

### **📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** *[KTU University Exam – June 2023]*

> **Discuss in detail the Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) method of hazard evaluation. Your answer must include:**
>
> **(a)** The historical background, purpose, and limitations of the Dow F&EI method. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The step-by-step procedure for calculating the F&EI, with a clear explanation of the Material Factor, General Process Hazards, and Special Process Hazards. Include the final formula and explain the significance of the Damage Factor and Loss Control Credit Factors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Background, Purpose & Limitations**

**Historical Background:**

The Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) was first developed and published by **The Dow Chemical Company, USA, in 1964** as a structured, numerical method to evaluate the relative fire and explosion hazard of chemical process units. It was created in response to the rapidly growing petrochemical industry in the post-WWII era, when insurance companies and safety engineers required a **standardized, quantitative tool** to:

- Rank process units by hazard potential
- Estimate probable property damage
- Justify capital expenditure on safety upgrades

Over the years, the methodology was revised five times. The current widely accepted standard is the **7th Edition (1994)**, which uses **metric units (meters)** and incorporates **loss control credit factors** for the first time.

**Purpose of Dow F&EI:**

1. **Hazard Quantification:** Convert qualitative hazards into a single numerical index.
2. **Prioritization:** Help safety managers rank units in a multi-unit plant for safety audits.
3. **Loss Estimation:** Quantify the **Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)** in monetary terms.
4. **Insurance Premium:** Used by underwriters to determine industrial fire insurance premiums.
5. **Regulatory Compliance:** Recognized by OSHA's PSM, Indian MSIHC Rules, and the Public Liability Insurance Act.
6. **Design Input:** Provides feedback for the layout of new process units (safe spacing, fire protection).

**Limitations:**

1. **Applicable only to chemical/process industries** — not suitable for mechanical, civil, or IT operations.
2. **Does not account for human error** directly; assumes a standard operator.
3. **Material Factor is tabulated**, requiring accurate substance identification.
4. **Damage Factor** must be read from a chart, which introduces subjective interpretation.
5. **Limited to fire and explosion** — does not cover toxic release, ergonomic, or environmental hazards comprehensively.
6. **Static methodology** — does not dynamically update with real-time process changes (e.g., IoT-based systems).
7. **Penalty factor selection** is partly subjective; different analysts may assign different GPH/SPH values.

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Procedure**

The Dow F&EI calculation is performed in the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Identify the Process Unit**

A *process unit* is any major item of process equipment (e.g., reactor, distillation column, compressor) and its associated piping, instrumentation, and structural supports.

**Step 2 — Determine the Material Factor (MF)**

The MF is the intrinsic energy release potential of the most hazardous substance. It is read from the **Dow 7th Edition Table 2**. For example, MF for **acetone = 16**, **propane = 21**, **hydrogen = 29**. For unknown substances, the MF is estimated from the heat of combustion and NFPA reactivity rating.

**Step 3 — Calculate General Process Hazards (GPH)**

GPH are **six categories** of generic penalties (exothermic reactions, endothermic processes, material handling, enclosed units, emergency access, drainage). The baseline is 1.0; each applicable penalty is added:

$$
GPH = 1.0 + \sum GPH_i
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Special Process Hazards (SPH)**

SPH are **twelve categories** of process-specific penalties (toxic materials, sub-atmospheric pressure, flammable-range operations, dust explosion, relief pressure, low temperature, quantity, corrosion, leakage, fired heaters, hot oil, rotating equipment):

$$
SPH = 1.0 + \sum SPH_i
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Process Unit Hazard Factor (F₃)**

$$
F_3 = MF \times GPH
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI)**

$$
\boxed{F\&EI = F_3 \times SPH = MF \times GPH \times SPH}
$$

**Step 7 — Read the Damage Factor (DF)**

DF is the **fraction of total equipment value in the exposure area** that would be destroyed. It is read from **Figure 1 of the Dow monograph** as a function of F₃ and F&EI.

**Significance of DF:** Translates the abstract F&EI into a **monetary loss percentage**, enabling direct dollar/rupee quantification.

**Step 8 — Calculate Radius of Exposure and Area of Exposure**

$$
R = 0.84 \times F\&EI \quad \text{(meters, 7th Ed.)}
$$

$$
A = \pi R^2
$$

**Step 9 — Calculate Base MPPD, MPDO, and BI**

$$
MPPD_{base} = A \times V_{eq} \times DF
$$

$$
BI_{base} = \frac{MPPD_{base}}{30} \times MPDO_{base} \times 0.70
$$

**Step 10 — Apply Loss Control Credit Factors**

The 7th Edition recognizes three categories of safety credits:

- **Process Control (C₁):** Alarms, interlocks, emergency shutdowns.
- **Material Isolation (C₂):** Block valves, remote-operated isolation, purging systems.
- **Fire Protection (C₃):** Sprinklers, foam systems, water spray, fire brigade.

$$
C = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3
$$

$$
MPPD_{actual} = MPPD_{base} \times C
$$

$$
MPDO_{actual} = MPDO_{base} \times C
$$

$$
BI_{actual} = \frac{MPPD_{actual}}{30} \times MPDO_{actual} \times 0.70
$$

**Significance of LCCF:** Rewards plants that have invested in engineered and procedural safety, providing a **realistic loss estimate** rather than a worst-case value.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes KTU Students Make in Dow F&EI Problems:**
>
> 1. **Using the WRONG radius formula:** The 7th Edition uses **R = 0.84 × F&EI (meters)**. The older 0.256 × F&EI (feet) formula is from the 2nd-4th editions. **Using the wrong one costs 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Adding instead of multiplying GPH/SPH:** Always **multiply**; the formula is $F\&EI = MF \times GPH \times SPH$. A common slip is writing GPH + SPH + MF.
> 3. **Forgetting the baseline 1.0:** Students often write GPH = 0.30 + 0.50 + 0.40 = 1.20 instead of 1.0 + 0.30 + 0.50 + 0.40 = 2.20.
> 4. **Wrong interpretation of Damage Factor:** DF is the **fractional** damage, not the absolute value. Multiplying by 100 in the final answer is a frequent error.
> 5. **Not converting replacement value:** If $V_{eq}$ is given per m², multiply by A first. If given as total value, multiply directly by DF.
> 6. **Forgetting the 0.70 factor** in the BI formula — this 70% represents the typical proportion of revenue exposed in process industries.
> 7. **Skipping the MPDO read from Fig. 4:** Always state "MPDO read from Dow Figure 4 = XX days" even if the examiner provides the value.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 1 hour before the KTU ESE.**

- ✅ **F&EI = MF × GPH × SPH** — this is the *single most important equation* on this topic.
- ✅ **Always use the 7th Edition metric formula** for radius: $R = 0.84 \times F\&EI$ in meters.
- ✅ **GPH has 6 categories, SPH has 12 categories** (memorize the list).
- ✅ **Baseline is 1.0** for both GPH and SPH; **add** the applicable penalties.
- ✅ **Damage Factor** is read from the Dow monograph chart (Figure 1); it is the *fraction* of property damaged.
- ✅ **Area of Exposure = πR²** is the circular zone of destruction.
- ✅ **MPDO** is read from Figure 4 of the Dow chart; it is the **Maximum Probable Days Outage**.
- ✅ **Business Interruption = (MPPD/30) × MPDO × 0.70** — the 0.70 is a *constant* per the 7th Edition.
- ✅ **Loss Control Credit Factor C = C₁ × C₂ × C₃** (always a product, never a sum).
- ✅ **Lower C = better safety systems = lower actual loss.**
- ✅ **Hazard bands:** Light (1–60), Moderate (61–96), Intermediate (97–127), Heavy (128–158), Severe (>158).
- ✅ **MF is tabulated, not calculated** — use the Dow 7th Edition Table 2 directly.
- ✅ **Multiply, never add, at every factor combination step.**
- ✅ **Final loss report must show both *base* and *actual* values** for full marks in KTU ESE.
- ✅ **Limitation to mention in Part A:** Dow F&EI is **only** for fire/explosion; not for toxicological, environmental, or ergonomic hazards.
- ✅ **7th Edition is metric** — never write feet in 2024 Scheme answers.
- ✅ **Application in India:** Public Liability Insurance Act 1991, MSIHC Rules 1989, Factories Act 1948.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preliminary Hazard Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT833 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Hazard Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a systematic, qualitative, team-based hazard identification technique performed during the earliest conceptual or design phase of a system, process, facility, or product, with the objective of identifying potential hazards, evaluating their initial severity and likelihood, and recommending preliminary design changes or safeguards to mitigate risks before detailed engineering begins.

According to the **U.S. Department of Defense Military Standard MIL-STD-882E** (System Safety), PHA is the mandatory first hazard analysis activity applied to a new system when limited detailed design information is available. The technique aligns with **ISO 12100:2010** (Safety of Machinery – General Principles for Design — Risk Assessment and Risk Reduction) and is a cornerstone of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 (Safety Analysis Techniques) under the **Industrial Safety Engineering** open elective.

### Key Terminology Used in KTU Examinations

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hazard** | Any real or potential condition that can cause injury, illness, death, or property/equipment damage |
| **Hazardous Event** | The specific occurrence in which a hazard is released (e.g., toxic gas release) |
| **Cause** | The combination of circumstances that lead to a hazardous event |
| **Effect** | The resulting consequence on personnel, equipment, mission, or environment |
| **Risk** | A function of **Severity** and **Probability** (often Severity × Likelihood) |
| **Mitigation** | Engineering or administrative control to reduce risk to an acceptable level |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Building a House"**
> Imagine you are an architect designing a new house. Before drawing detailed plumbing, wiring, or structural blueprints, you walk through the concept with a builder, an electrician, a fire-safety officer, and a neighbor. You ask:
> - "Where might someone slip on stairs?" (Slip hazard)
> - "Could a kitchen fire spread to the upper floor?" (Fire hazard)
> - "Is the electrical panel near the water tank?" (Electrocution hazard)
>
> You write these down in a simple table with rough ratings like *High/Medium/Low* and propose quick fixes — non-slip tiles, a fire door, relocating the panel. This *preliminary* discussion **before detailed drawings** is exactly what PHA does for industrial systems.

**Why PHA is called "Preliminary":**
It is performed **before** detailed analyses like **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)**, **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**, or **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**. It is a *coarse but fast* filter that highlights areas needing deeper study.

### When PHA is Performed (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trigger Points for PHA:**
> 1. New system, facility, or product at the **concept stage**
> 2. Major modification to an existing process
> 3. End of **conceptual design** phase (≈ 10–30% design maturity)
> 4. Introduction of new chemicals, energies, or operating conditions
> 5. As an input to the **System Safety Program Plan (SSPP)**

---

## 1.3 Standard PHA Risk Metrics (MIL-STD-882E Reference)

The following **Severity Categories** and **Probability Levels** are universally accepted for KTU 2024 Scheme PHA questions:

**Hazard Severity Categories:**

| Category | Description | Mishap Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **I — Catastrophic** | Death, system loss, severe environmental damage | Could result in fatality |
| **II — Critical** | Severe injury, occupational illness, major damage | Permanent partial disability |
| **III — Marginal** | Minor injury, minor system damage | First-aid or minor reversible harm |
| **IV — Negligible** | Less than minor injury, no system damage | Negligible consequence |

**Probability Levels:**

| Level | Description | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Frequent** | Likely to occur frequently | ≥ 1 in 10 operations |
| **B — Probable** | Will occur several times in system life | 1 in 10 to 1 in 100 |
| **C — Occasional** | Likely to occur sometime in system life | 1 in 100 to 1 in 1000 |
| **D — Remote** | Unlikely but possible to occur | 1 in 1000 to 1 in 10,000 |
| **E — Improbable** | So unlikely that it can be assumed occurrence may not be experienced | < 1 in 10,000 |

The standard **Risk Assessment Code (RAC)** matrix combining the above:

| | Catastrophic (I) | Critical (II) | Marginal (III) | Negligible (IV) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A — Frequent** | **1** | **1** | **2** | **3** |
| **B — Probable** | **1** | **2** | **3** | **4** |
| **C — Occasional** | **2** | **3** | **3** | **4** |
| **D — Remote** | **3** | **3** | **4** | **4** |
| **E — Improbable** | **4** | **4** | **4** | **4** |

**RAC Interpretation:**
- **RAC 1** = Unacceptable / Must be eliminated
- **RAC 2** = Undesirable / Must be reduced by controls
- **RAC 3** = Acceptable with management approval
- **RAC 4** = Acceptable as-is
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose and Objectives of PHA

The PHA serves **four primary engineering purposes** which examiners frequently test:

1. **Hazard Identification** — Reveal all foreseeable hazards early.
2. **Risk Estimation** — Assign rough Severity × Probability ratings.
3. **Mitigation Recommendation** — Suggest design changes, safeguards, or procedural controls.
4. **Scoping Detailed Analysis** — Direct HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, or What-If analyses toward the highest-risk areas.

> [!NOTE]
> PHA is **qualitative** (sometimes semi-quantitative) — it does **not** use complex mathematical fault trees or probability distributions. It relies on **engineering judgment** and **checklist analysis** combined with **What-If analysis**.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step PHA Methodology (KTU Board Standard)

The PHA process consists of **six sequential steps**:

### Step 1 — Define the System Boundary and Scope

Clearly state:
- The physical system or process under review
- Operating modes (start-up, normal, shut-down, emergency, maintenance)
- Environmental conditions (temperature, pressure, humidity, altitude)
- Interfaces with adjacent systems

### Step 2 — Assemble the PHA Team

A multi-disciplinary team typically includes:
- **System / Process Engineer** (design knowledge)
- **Safety Engineer** (hazard expertise)
- **Operations / Plant Operator** (practical experience)
- **Maintenance Engineer** (failure modes)
- **Subject Matter Expert** (chemistry, electrical, instrumentation, etc.)
- **Facilitator / Leader** (often a certified safety professional)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Board Note: PHA is a **team exercise** — it is **not** done by a single individual. The team brings diverse perspectives that catch hazards a single engineer would miss.

### Step 3 — Identify Hazards

Three principal hazard-identification inputs are used:

1. **Checklist Analysis** — A pre-existing list of generic hazards (electrical, mechanical, thermal, chemical, radiation, ergonomic, environmental) is used as a memory aid.
2. **What-If Analysis** — The team brainstorms: *"What if the pump fails?"* / *"What if the operator opens valve V-101 at start-up?"* / *"What if power is lost?"*
3. **Hazard Categories** — Review by energy domain: kinetic, potential, electrical, chemical, thermal, radiation, biological, ergonomic.

### Step 4 — Evaluate Causes and Effects

For each identified hazard, document:
- **Hazard description** (e.g., release of chlorine gas)
- **Potential causes** (e.g., corrosion, gasket failure, operator error)
- **Worst credible effect** on personnel, equipment, mission, environment

### Step 5 — Assign Risk (Severity × Probability)

Using the **RAC matrix** (Section 1.3), the team assigns a Risk Assessment Code to each hazardous event.

### Step 6 — Recommend Mitigation Measures

For every hazard with **RAC 1 or RAC 2**, the team must propose:
- **Engineering Controls** (design changes, relief valves, alarms, interlocks)
- **Administrative Controls** (procedures, training, signage)
- **PPE** (last line of defence)

The hazard is then **re-rated** post-mitigation. If still unacceptable, the cycle repeats.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises all quantitative/qualitative expressions used in PHA calculations. KTU examinations expect familiarity with the **RAC matrix** and the basic risk equation.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Score (semi-quantitative) | $R = S \times P$ | $R$ = Risk index (1–25), $S$ = Severity (1–4), $P$ = Probability (1–5) |
| 2 | Severity Category (MIL-STD-882E) | $S \in \{I, II, III, IV\}$ | Roman numerals or numeric {4,3,2,1} |
| 3 | Probability Level | $P \in \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | Alphabetic or numeric {5,4,3,2,1} |
| 4 | Hazard Rate (quantitative extension) | $h = \dfrac{\text{Number of hazardous events}}{\text{Unit time}}$ | events/year, events/10⁶ hrs |
| 5 | Residual Risk | $R_{res} = S \times P_{post\text{-}mitigation}$ | Re-evaluated after safeguards |
| 6 | Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \dfrac{R_{initial}}{R_{residual}}$ | Dimensionless ratio ≥ 1 |
| 7 | As Low As Reasonably Practicable | $ALARP$ | Test of cost vs. risk reduction |
| 8 | Hazard Identification Index | $HII = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Sum of risk scores for all hazards |
| 9 | Risk Reduction Percentage | $\%RR = \left(1 - \dfrac{R_{res}}{R_{init}}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage reduction in risk |
| 10 | Acceptable Threshold | $R \le R_{acceptable}$ | Defined by company policy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** Although PHA is primarily qualitative, the **semi-quantitative index** $R = S \times P$ is widely used in KTU 2024 Scheme numerical questions. The maximum risk is $4 \times 5 = 20$ (or 25 on a 5×5 scale).

---

## 2.4 Advantages and Limitations of PHA

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Almost every KTU 14-mark question on PHA includes a 4–5 mark sub-question on *"Discuss the merits and limitations of PHA."* Memorize the following:

### Advantages

1. **Early detection** — Hazards caught at concept stage are *100× to 1000× cheaper* to fix than those caught after construction.
2. **Low cost and quick** — Requires no detailed design data, runs in 1–3 days.
3. **Provides a roadmap** for subsequent HAZOP/FMEA/FTA studies.
4. **Multi-disciplinary insight** brings diverse expertise.
5. **Documentation** forms the basis of the **System Safety Program Plan (SSPP)**.

### Limitations

1. **Qualitative** — Cannot give exact failure probabilities (use Fault Tree Analysis for that).
2. **Highly dependent on team experience** — A weak team misses hazards.
3. **Limited to conceptual design** — Cannot resolve complex interaction of subsystems.
4. **No node-by-node** systematic review (use HAZOP for that).
5. **Subjective ratings** — Severity and probability scoring may vary between teams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — PHA of a Chlorine Storage System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a typical KTU 14-mark style worked example. Memorize the structure: **System → Hazards → Causes → Effects → Severity → Probability → RAC → Mitigation → Re-Rating**.

### System Description
A facility stores liquid chlorine in a 1-tonne pressurized vessel at **6 bar** and ambient temperature. The system includes a storage tank, feed line, vaporizer, control valves, and an emergency shutdown (ESD) system. Plant is located 500 m from a residential colony.

### Step 1: System Boundary
- Boundary: Tank → Pump → Vaporizer → Process line
- Modes: Normal, Start-up, Shutdown, Maintenance
- Ambient: 30–45 °C, Humid coastal climate

### Step 2: Team
Process Engineer, Safety Officer, Plant Operator, Maintenance Engineer, Chlorine SME, Facilitator.

### Step 3–6: PHA Worksheet (Excerpt)

| # | Hazard | Cause | Effect | Sev | Prob | Initial RAC | Mitigation | Res Sev | Res Prob | Res RAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cl₂ gas leak from tank flange | Gasket degradation, over-pressurization | Toxic exposure, fatality within 50 m | I (Cat) | C (Occas) | **2** | Double gasket, continuous gas monitor, emergency scrubber, ESD | I | E | **4** |
| 2 | Tank overpressure | Vaporizer steam failure, blocked vent | BLEVE/explosion | I | D | **3** | Pressure relief valve, rupture disk, level interlocks | I | E | **4** |
| 3 | Pump mechanical seal failure | Wear, dry running | Cl₂ release to pump room | II | C | **3** | Seal pot monitoring, dual mechanical seal, local exhaust | II | E | **4** |
| 4 | Operator exposure during manual sampling | Splashing, inadequate PPE | Chemical burn, respiratory irritation | II | B | **2** | Closed-loop sampling, training, full-face SCBA, SOP | III | D | **4** |
| 5 | Lightning strike on tank | No lightning protection | Tank rupture, major Cl₂ release | I | E | **4** | Lightning arrester, earthing grid | I | E | **4** |
| 6 | Vehicular impact on yard piping | Forklift collision | Pipe rupture, gas release | II | D | **3** | Crash barriers, pipe routing under road slab, signage | III | E | **4** |

### Step 7: Derivation of the Risk Score Formula

The semi-quantitative risk index used by most teams is:

$$R = S \times P$$

where $S$ is the numeric severity value and $P$ is the numeric probability value.

For Hazard #1 (above):
- $S = 4$ (Catastrophic, Category I)
- $P = 3$ (Occasional, Level C)
- $R_{initial} = 4 \times 3 = 12$ → **RAC 2** (Undesirable)

After mitigation (continuous gas monitor + ESD + double gasket):
- $S_{residual} = 4$ (severity unchanged — consequence of a leak is still catastrophic)
- $P_{residual} = 1$ (Improbable, Level E)
- $R_{residual} = 4 \times 1 = 4$ → **RAC 4** (Acceptable)

### Step 8: Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) Calculation

$$RRF = \dfrac{R_{initial}}{R_{residual}} = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3$$

This means the proposed safeguards reduce the *risk index* by a factor of 3 (or 66.7% reduction).

### Step 9: Percentage Risk Reduction

$$\%RR = \left(1 - \dfrac{R_{res}}{R_{init}}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{4}{12}\right) \times 100 = 66.67\%$$

### Step 10: Hazard Identification Index (HII) — Plant-Level

Summing the initial risk scores for all six hazards:

$$HII = 12 + 9 + 6 + 6 + 4 + 6 = 43$$

After implementing all proposed mitigations:

$$HII_{res} = 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 24$$

**Plant-level percentage risk reduction:**

$$\%RR_{plant} = \left(1 - \dfrac{24}{43}\right) \times 100 = 44.19\%$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Risk Ranking Question

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU 3-Mark Style:** *"For the following hazards, compute the Risk Index using $R = S \times P$ and recommend whether the system is acceptable. Use a 4×5 matrix where $R \le 6$ is acceptable, $7$–$12$ is ALARP, and $> 12$ is unacceptable."*

| Hazard | $S$ | $P$ | $R$ | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1: Steam burn from open valve | 3 | 4 | 12 | ALARP — improve guarding |
| H2: Electrical shock from panel | 4 | 2 | 8 | ALARP — add RCD |
| H3: Slip on oily floor | 2 | 5 | 10 | ALARP — drainage + sign |
| H4: Crane drop on personnel | 4 | 1 | 4 | Acceptable |
| H5: Confined-space asphyxiation | 4 | 3 | 12 | ALARP — gas testing + SCBA |

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- Hazards with $R > 6$ require ALARP demonstrations — the design team must show that *further risk reduction is impracticable or grossly disproportionate in cost*.
- $R > 12$ risks (none here) would be **unacceptable** in any modern facility.

---

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — PHA vs Other Safety Techniques (KTU Favourite)

| Feature | PHA | HAZOP | FMEA | FTA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage of use** | Concept / early design | Detailed design | Detailed design / operation | Detailed design / operation |
| **Type** | Qualitative | Qualitative | Qualitative / Semi-quantitative | Quantitative |
| **Driving question** | "What can go wrong?" | "What if deviation occurs?" | "How can this part fail?" | "How can top event occur?" |
| **Team size** | 4–8 | 5–10 | 3–6 | 3–6 |
| **Output** | Hazard list + RAC | Node deviations | Failure modes + RPN | Cut sets + probability |
| **Time** | 1–3 days | 1–4 weeks | 2–5 days | 1–2 weeks |
| **Best for** | Scoping | Process plants | Mechanical / electrical | Complex high-consequence |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners commonly ask: *"Differentiate between PHA and HAZOP"* (5 marks) or *"When would you choose PHA over FMEA?"* (3 marks). The above table covers both.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PHA Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start PHA Project]) --> stepB[Define System Boundary and Scope]
    stepB --> stepC[Assemble Multi-Disciplinary PHA Team]
    stepC --> stepD{Gather System Information}
    stepD --> stepE[Review P&ID Process Flow Diagrams]
    stepD --> stepF[Review Operating Procedures]
    stepD --> stepG[Review Past Incident Records]
    stepE --> stepH
    stepF --> stepH
    stepG --> stepH[Identify Hazards using Checklists and What-If]
    stepH --> stepI[Document Causes and Worst-Credible Effects]
    stepI --> stepJ[Assign Severity Category I to IV]
    stepJ --> stepK[Assign Probability Level A to E]
    stepK --> stepL[Determine Risk Assessment Code RAC 1 to 4]
    stepL --> stepM{RAC Greater Than 2}
    stepM -- Yes --> stepN[Recommend Mitigation Controls]
    stepN --> stepO[Re-Evaluate Residual Risk]
    stepO --> stepP{Residual RAC Acceptable}
    stepP -- No --> stepN
    stepP -- Yes --> stepQ[Document PHA Worksheet and Report]
    stepM -- No --> stepQ
    stepQ --> stepR[Update System Safety Program Plan]
    stepR --> endS([End of PHA Phase])
```

## 4.2 Risk Assessment Code (RAC) Matrix Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SeverityColumn [Severity Categories]
        sevI[Catastrophic I]
        sevII[Critical II]
        sevIII[Marginal III]
        sevIV[Negligible IV]
    end
    subgraph ProbabilityRow [Probability Levels]
        probA[Frequent A]
        probB[Probable B]
        probC[Occasional C]
        probD[Remote D]
        probE[Improbable E]
    end
    subgraph RiskCodes [Risk Assessment Code Output]
        rac1[Code 1 Unacceptable Eliminate]
        rac2[Code 2 Undesirable Reduce]
        rac3[Code 3 Acceptable with Approval]
        rac4[Code 4 Acceptable As-Is]
    end
    sevI --> rac1
    probA --> rac1
    sevII --> rac2
    probB --> rac2
    sevIII --> rac3
    probC --> rac3
    sevIV --> rac4
    probD --> rac4
    probE --> rac4
```

## 4.3 PHA Worksheet — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WorksheetColumns [PHA Worksheet Columns]
        colA[Column 1 Hazard Description]
        colB[Column 2 Potential Cause]
        colC[Column 3 Worst Credible Effect]
        colD[Column 4 Severity Category]
        colE[Column 5 Probability Level]
        colF[Column 6 Initial RAC]
        colG[Column 7 Recommended Mitigation]
        colH[Column 8 Residual Severity]
        colI[Column 9 Residual Probability]
        colJ[Column 10 Final RAC]
        colK[Column 11 Remarks and References]
    end
    colA --> colB --> colC --> colD --> colE --> colF --> colG --> colH --> colI --> colJ --> colK
```

## 4.4 PHA Inputs and Outputs — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Inputs [PHA Inputs]
        in1[System Description and P and ID]
        in2[Operating Procedures and SOPs]
        in3[Material Safety Data Sheets MSDS]
        in4[Past Incident Database]
        in5[Regulatory Standards and Codes]
        in6[Checklists from Similar Systems]
    end
    subgraph PHAEngine [Preliminary Hazard Analysis Process]
        engineA[Qualitative Team Review]
        engineB[What-If Brainstorming]
        engineC[Checklist Application]
    end
    subgraph Outputs [PHA Outputs]
        out1[Hazard Register Worksheet]
        out2[Risk Assessment Codes RAC]
        out3[Mitigation Action List]
        out4[Recommended Design Changes]
        out5[Input to HAZOP and FMEA]
    end
    in1 --> engineA
    in2 --> engineA
    in3 --> engineB
    in4 --> engineB
    in5 --> engineC
    in6 --> engineC
    engineA --> out1
    engineB --> out2
    engineC --> out3
    out1 --> out4
    out2 --> out5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"What is Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)? State any two situations where PHA is preferred over other hazard analysis techniques."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) is a systematic, team-based, qualitative hazard identification and risk evaluation technique performed during the early conceptual design phase of a system, process, or facility. (2 marks)
>
> **Two situations where PHA is preferred:** (1 mark each)
> 1. When a *new* system or product is at the **concept stage** and detailed design data is not yet available.
> 2. When a *major modification* is proposed to an existing facility, and a quick scoping of new hazards is needed before detailed HAZOP/FMEA is performed.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*"Differentiate between a hazard and a hazardous event. Give one example of each in a process plant."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A **hazard** is a *condition* or *source* with the inherent potential to cause harm (e.g., pressurised chlorine gas in a storage vessel). A **hazardous event** is the *occurrence* in which that hazard is actually released and causes harm (e.g., rupture of the chlorine storage vessel releasing 1 tonne of toxic gas). (2 marks)
>
> **Examples:** (1 mark)
> - Hazard → Stored liquid ammonia at 10 bar.
> - Hazardous event → Ammonia line rupture, exposing the operator to toxic vapours.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by **confusing PHA with HAZOP or FMEA**. Always state explicitly that PHA is a *concept-stage qualitative* technique while HAZOP/FMEA require *detailed design data*.
> - In PHA worksheet questions, students often forget the **residual-risk re-rating column**. Always show *both* initial and final RAC.
> - Do **not** write "severity × probability" without stating the **category and level** from MIL-STD-882E. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unsourced ratings.
> - When asked for "limitations," students list only 2–3 points. Examiners expect *at least 4 distinct limitations* for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice A]**

*(a)* Explain the objectives, steps, and team composition of Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) with a suitable example. (7 Marks)

*(b)* Construct a PHA worksheet for a **LPG bullet storage installation** and compute the **Risk Index** $R = S \times P$ for any three identified hazards. Recommend suitable mitigation for hazards with RAC = 1 or 2. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Objectives of PHA** (2 marks):
1. Identify all foreseeable hazards early in the conceptual design.
2. Assign initial severity and probability to each hazard.
3. Recommend preliminary mitigation measures.
4. Direct the scope of subsequent detailed analyses (HAZOP, FMEA, FTA).

**Steps of PHA** (3 marks):
1. Define system boundary, scope, and operating modes.
2. Assemble a multi-disciplinary team (safety, process, operations, maintenance, SME, facilitator).
3. Identify hazards using **checklists**, **what-if analysis**, and **hazard categories** (chemical, thermal, electrical, mechanical, etc.).
4. For each hazard, list the **cause** and the **worst credible effect**.
5. Assign **Severity Category** (I–IV) and **Probability Level** (A–E) using the MIL-STD-882E matrix.
6. Compute the **Risk Assessment Code (RAC = S × P)** and recommend **engineering / administrative / PPE controls** for any hazard with RAC 1 or 2.
7. Re-rate the residual risk after mitigation. Document the PHA worksheet and integrate it into the System Safety Program Plan.

**Team Composition** (1 mark):
- Facilitator (certified safety professional)
- System/Process Engineer
- Safety Engineer
- Plant Operator
- Maintenance Engineer
- Subject Matter Expert (e.g., chemist, instrumentation)

**Example** (1 mark): PHA of a chlorine storage yard — identifying gas-leak hazard, assigning RAC 2, and recommending double gaskets + gas detectors + emergency scrubber.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**LPG Bullet Storage — System Description:**
Two 10-tonne horizontal LPG bullets, 17 °C, 8.5 bar, with pressure relief valves, ESD system, and truck-loading gantry. Site is 800 m from a residential area.

**PHA Worksheet (3 hazards):**

| # | Hazard | Cause | Effect | $S$ | $P$ | $R_{init}$ | Initial RAC | Mitigation | $R_{res}$ | Final RAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LPG vapour cloud explosion at bullet flange | Gasket failure, lightning | Vapour cloud explosion, fatality | 4 (I) | 3 (C) | 12 | **2** | Double gasket, gas detector, ESD, lightning arrester | 4 | **4** |
| 2 | Overpressure in bullet | Blocked PRV, fire exposure | BLEVE, catastrophic explosion | 4 (I) | 2 (D) | 8 | **3** | Dual PRV, firewater spray deluge, level interlocks | 4 | **4** |
| 3 | Truck collision with bullet during loading | Driver error, no barriers | Bullet puncture, fireball | 4 (I) | 2 (D) | 8 | **3** | Crash barriers, wheel chocks, anti-collision system, speed limits | 4 | **4** |

**Calculations** (2 marks):
- Hazard 1: $R_{init} = 4 \times 3 = 12$ → RAC 2 (Undesirable)
- Hazard 2: $R_{init} = 4 \times 2 = 8$ → RAC 3 (Acceptable with management approval)
- Hazard 3: $R_{init} = 4 \times 2 = 8$ → RAC 3

**Mitigation Summary** (2 marks):
For Hazard 1 (RAC 2), the team must reduce risk: install double-gasket, continuous hydrocarbon gas monitor with 10% LEL alarm, automatic ESD, and a lightning protection system. After mitigation, $R_{res} = 4 \times 1 = 4$ → RAC 4 (Acceptable).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Choice B]**

*(a)* Compare PHA with HAZOP and FMEA in terms of *stage of use, methodology, output, and limitations*. (7 Marks)

*(b)* For a **boiler system** (diesel-fired, 1 TPH, 10 bar), perform a PHA listing at least **five hazards**, assign Severity and Probability, and compute the **Hazard Identification Index (HII)** before and after mitigation. Comment on the **percentage risk reduction**. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Aspect | PHA | HAZOP | FMEA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage of use** | Concept / early design | Detailed design / commissioning | Detailed design / operation |
| **Methodology** | Checklist + What-If + team brainstorming | Systematic node-by-node deviation analysis (e.g., "no flow", "more pressure") | Item-by-item failure-mode analysis with RPN |
| **Output** | Hazard register + RAC | Cause-consequence matrix per deviation | Failure mode list + RPN ranking |
| **Limitations** | Subjective, qualitative, no probability | Time-consuming, requires complete P&ID | Focuses on single-component failure, ignores combinations |

*Elaboration* (1 mark): PHA is the *coarsest* and *earliest* of the three; it acts as a **scoping tool** that highlights where HAZOP and FMEA should be applied in detail.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Boiler System Description:** Diesel-fired package boiler, 1 TPH steam, 10 bar working pressure, with feedwater pump, fuel system, burner, and stack.

**PHA Worksheet (5 hazards):**

| # | Hazard | Cause | Effect | $S$ | $P$ | $R_{init}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Boiler explosion due to low water level | Level sensor failure, operator inattention | Catastrophic vessel rupture | 4 (I) | 2 (D) | 8 |
| 2 | Fire due to diesel leak at burner | Hose crack, valve failure | Furnace fire/explosion | 4 (I) | 3 (C) | 12 |
| 3 | Burn injury from hot surfaces | Missing insulation | Operator burn injury | 3 (II) | 4 (B) | 12 |
| 4 | CO poisoning from incomplete combustion | Poor air-fuel ratio | Toxic exposure in boiler room | 3 (II) | 2 (D) | 6 |
| 5 | Noise-induced hearing loss (> 90 dBA) | Burner + steam noise | Permanent hearing damage | 2 (III) | 4 (B) | 8 |

**Initial HII Calculation** (2 marks):

$$HII_{init} = 8 + 12 + 12 + 6 + 8 = 46$$

**Recommended Mitigations:**
- Hazard 1: Independent low-water cutoff (LWCO), redundant level transmitter, alarm + burner trip.
- Hazard 2: Flame scanner, double-walled fuel line, leak detector, ESD.
- Hazard 3: 50 mm mineral-wool insulation, warning signage, locked maintenance access.
- Hazard 4: Combustion analyser, O₂ trim control, ventilation upgrade.
- Hazard 5: Acoustic enclosure, hearing protection (PPE), noise monitoring.

**Residual Risk Ratings:**

| # | $S_{res}$ | $P_{res}$ | $R_{res}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 1 | 4 |
| 2 | 4 | 1 | 4 |
| 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| 4 | 3 | 1 | 3 |
| 5 | 2 | 2 | 4 |

**Residual HII** (1 mark):

$$HII_{res} = 4 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 4 = 19$$

**Percentage Risk Reduction** (1 mark):

$$\%RR = \left(1 - \dfrac{19}{46}\right) \times 100 = 58.70\%$$

**Comment** (1 mark): The proposed mitigation strategy reduces the plant-level hazard identification index by **58.7%**, demonstrating effective ALARP-level control. Hazards 1 and 2 (initially catastrophic) are now re-rated to RAC 4 with the proposed interlocks, but the design must ensure the LWCO and flame scanner are *independent* and *diverse* to meet IEC 61508 SIL requirements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Board Crammer):**
> - ✅ PHA = **Preliminary** (concept stage), **Hazard** (identification), **Analysis** (qualitative/team-based).
> - ✅ Based on **MIL-STD-882E** and aligned with **ISO 12100:2010**.
> - ✅ Severity categories: **I (Catastrophic), II (Critical), III (Marginal), IV (Negligible)**.
> - ✅ Probability levels: **A (Frequent), B (Probable), C (Occasional), D (Remote), E (Improbable)**.
> - ✅ **Risk Assessment Code (RAC) 1–4**: 1 = Unacceptable, 2 = Undesirable, 3 = Acceptable with approval, 4 = Acceptable.
> - ✅ **Semi-quantitative risk score** = $R = S \times P$ (max 20 on 4×5 scale).
> - ✅ **6 steps**: Define scope → Team → Identify hazards → Causes/effects → RAC → Mitigation & re-rate.
> - ✅ **Tools used**: Checklists, What-If, Hazard categories.
> - ✅ **Always re-rate residual risk** after mitigation — this is the most-skipped step in KTU scripts.
> - ✅ **HII (Hazard Identification Index)** = $\sum R_i$ at plant level; useful for comparing alternative designs.
> - ✅ PHA is the **scoping tool** for HAZOP/FMEA/FTA — it does not replace them.
> - ✅ **Team** = 4–8 multi-disciplinary members; *never* done by a single engineer.
> - ✅ **Limitations**: Qualitative, subjective, dependent on team experience, no node-by-node review.
> - ✅ **ALARP test** must be applied to all hazards in RAC 2 or 3 before sign-off.
> - ✅ Documents are integrated into the **System Safety Program Plan (SSPP)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
