# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating FAR (Fatal Accident Rate)

**Problem Statement (KTU Exam Style):**

> A manufacturing company employs **450 workers** working **8 hours per day, 300 days per year**. During the year, there were **2 fatal accidents**. Calculate the **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** and interpret the result against the international benchmark.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data**

$$N_f = 2\ \text{fatalities}$$
$$\text{Workers} = 450$$
$$\text{Hours/day} = 8$$
$$\text{Days/year} = 300$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Total Man-Hours Worked Per Year**

$$T_h = \text{Workers} \times \text{Hours/day} \times \text{Days/year}$$
$$T_h = 450 \times 8 \times 300$$
$$T_h = 1{,}080{,}000\ \text{man-hours per year}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the FAR Formula**

$$FAR = \frac{N_f \times 10^8}{T_h}$$
$$FAR = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{1{,}080{,}000}$$
$$FAR = \frac{200{,}000{,}000}{1{,}080{,}000}$$
$$FAR \approx 185.19$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result**

$$FAR \approx 185$$

**Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Table — Benchmark FAR Values**

| Industry / Region | Typical FAR |
|---|---|
| Indian Manufacturing (Average) | 100 – 250 |
| UK Manufacturing (Historical) | ~4 |
| US Chemical Industry | ~5 |
| World-Class Safety Performance | < 1 |
| **This Company** | **185 (Below Average — High Risk)** |

**Conclusion:** The company FAR of **185** is **significantly higher than world-class benchmarks** and indicates a need for immediate safety system overhaul, including enhanced training, engineering controls, and management commitment.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Risk Score Using Fine-Kinney Method

**Problem Statement:**

> In a metal fabrication workshop, workers operate a **hydraulic press** for **2–4 hours per day**. A recent near-miss revealed finger crush injury potential. Use the **Fine-Kinney Method** to compute the risk score and recommend control measures.

**Step 1 — Assign Ratings**

| Parameter | Rating (Value) | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Probability (P)** | **6** (Could occur) | Pinch points present, no light curtain |
| **Severity (S)** | **5** (Serious — fracture/amputation) | Crush injury possible |
| **Exposure (E)** | **6** (Daily, 2–4 hours) | Operated every shift |

**Step 2 — Apply Fine-Kinney Formula**

$$R = P \times S \times E$$
$$R = 6 \times 5 \times 6$$
$$R = 180$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Risk Score Classification**

| Risk Score Range | Risk Level | Action |
|---|---|---|
| R > 400 | Very High | Stop activity immediately |
| 200 < R ≤ 400 | High | Immediate corrective action |
| 70 < R ≤ 200 | Substantial | Correction needed |
| 20 < R ≤ 70 | Moderate | Attention & control |
| R ≤ 20 | Low | Acceptable |

**Step 4 — Interpret**

$$R = 180 \Rightarrow \textbf{Substantial Risk}$$

**Conclusion & Recommendation:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommended Hierarchy of Controls Application:**
> 1. **Engineering Control:** Install two-hand trip control + light curtain (highest priority).
> 2. **Administrative Control:** Develop SOP for press operation; train all operators.
> 3. **PPE:** Issue cut-resistant gloves and safety footwear.
> 4. **Verification:** Re-assess R after controls; target R ≤ 20.

**Step 5 — Calculate Risk Reduction Factor**

$$\text{Risk Reduction Factor} = \frac{R_{before}}{R_{after}}$$

After engineering control installation, probability reduces to P = 1, severity to S = 5, exposure to E = 6:

$$R_{after} = 1 \times 5 \times 6 = 30$$
$$RRF = \frac{180}{30} = 6$$

**Conclusion:** The controls achieved a **6-fold risk reduction** — substantial improvement, though continued monitoring is needed.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — LTIFR Calculation

**Problem Statement:**

> A construction project employs **1,200 workers** working **9 hours/day for 250 days/year**. During the year, **8 lost-time injuries** resulted in a total of **120 lost workdays**. Calculate:
> (a) **Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)**
> (b) **Severity Rate**

**Step 1 — Compute Total Man-Hours**

$$T_h = 1200 \times 9 \times 250 = 2{,}700{,}000\ \text{man-hours}$$

**Step 2 — Compute LTIFR**

$$LTIFR = \frac{\text{Number of LTI} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-hours}}$$
$$LTIFR = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{2{,}700{,}000}$$
$$LTIFR = \frac{8{,}000{,}000}{2{,}700{,}000}$$
$$LTIFR \approx 2.96\ \text{per million man-hours}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Severity Rate**

$$SR = \frac{\text{Lost Days} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-hours}}$$
$$SR = \frac{120 \times 10^6}{2{,}700{,}000}$$
$$SR = \frac{120{,}000{,}000}{2{,}700{,}000}$$
$$SR \approx 44.44$$

**Step 4 — Benchmark Interpretation**

| Indicator | Calculated | Indian Construction Avg | World-Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| LTIFR | 2.96 | ~5–8 | < 1 |
| Severity Rate | 44.44 | ~80–120 | < 20 |

**Conclusion:** Performance is **better than the Indian average but not yet world-class**. Continue monitoring and target LTIFR < 1.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Risk Assessment Method

| Method | Best For | Data Required | Time Required | Cost | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Checklist Analysis** | Routine operations, screening | Past incidents, standards | Hours | Low | High |
| **What-If Analysis** | New processes, brainstorming | Subject matter experts | 1–2 days | Low | Medium |
| **JSA / JHA** | Specific jobs, manual tasks | Job observations | Hours–Days | Low | High |
| **HAZOP** | Process plants (chemical, petrochemical) | P&IDs, node breakdown | 1–3 weeks | High | Very High |
| **FMEA** | Equipment & design failure | Failure data, MTBF | 1–2 weeks | Medium | High |
| **FTA** | Complex system reliability | Logic gates, probabilities | 1–4 weeks | High | Medium |
| **ETA (Event Tree)** | Post-initiator consequences | Initiator frequency | 1–2 weeks | Medium | Medium |
| **Bow-Tie** | Major hazard management | Threats & consequences | Days | Medium | Very High |
| **QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment)** | High-hazard industries | Comprehensive data | Months | Very High | Low (advanced topic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Exam Trigger Phrases:**
> - *"Discuss in detail HAZOP for a chemical reactor"* → Explain guide words + node + deviation.
> - *"Apply JSA to a welding operation"* → Break job into steps; identify hazards per step.
> - *"Compare FMEA and FTA"* → Use the analytical matrix above as the foundation.

## 3.5 Derivative Insight — Heinrich's Domino Theory & Documentation Link

```
┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────┐
│ ANCESTRY │ ──▶ │ FAULTY PERSON │ ──▶ │UNSAFE ACT│ ──▶ │   ACCIDENT   │ ──▶ │  INJURY  │
│ / SOCIAL │     │ (Personal     │     │ / UNSAFE │     │ (Loss Event)  │     │          │
│ENVIRONMENT│     │  Factors)    │     │ CONDITION│     │              │     │          │
└──────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────┘
     ↓                  ↓                    ↓                  ↓                  ↓
 [Policy Doc.]    [Training Records]   [SOP / Checklist]   [Incident Report]  [Medical Record]
```

**Interpretation:** Each "domino" in Heinrich's 1931 theory maps to a specific *type* of safety document. Removing the middle domino (the unsafe act/condition) through documentation-controlled procedures is the most effective intervention — **removing one domino collapses the entire chain.**

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Incident Investigation Workflow (RCA Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Incident[Incident Occurs] --> Secure[Secure the Scene]
    Secure --> Notify[Notify Supervisor and Safety Officer]
    Notify --> Form[Form Investigation Team]
    Form --> Collect[Collect Evidence Photographs Witness Statements]
    Collect --> Timeline[Establish Chronological Timeline]
    Timeline --> Immediate[Identify Immediate Causes Unsafe Acts and Conditions]
    Immediate --> Root[Apply 5-Why or Fishbone to Find Root Cause]
    Root --> Recommend[Develop CAPA Corrective and Preventive Actions]
    Recommend --> Implement[Implement CAPA with Target Dates]
    Implement --> Verify[Verify Effectiveness of CAPA]
    Verify --> Effective{Issue Resolved?}
    Effective -->|Yes| Close[Close the Investigation]
    Effective -->|No| Root
    Close --> Lessons[Share Lessons Learned Across Organization]
    Lessons --> Update[Update Risk Register and Procedures]
    Update --> Incident
```

**Reading the Workflow:** Note the **feedback loop from "Verify" back to "Root Cause"** — this ensures that ineffective CAPAs are re-examined, not merely closed administratively.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question]**
> *"List any six components of a Safety Procedure document."*

**Model Answer (Point-Wise):**

1. **Document Control Header** — Number, revision, date, approver.
2. **Purpose and Scope** — Why and to what the procedure applies.
3. **References** — Statutory and internal references.
4. **Definitions** — Glossary of terms.
5. **Responsibilities** — Roles of operators, supervisors, safety officers.
6. **Procedure Steps** — Numbered, sequential, action-oriented.
7. **Emergency Provisions** — Actions during abnormal conditions.
8. **Revision History** — Change tracking.

*(Any six points — full 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023, Model Question]**
> *"Explain the significance of the 'DAD' principle in record keeping."*

**Model Answer:**

The **DAD Principle** ensures that safety records are *Documented, Approved, and Detained* (retained) in a manner that satisfies both legal and operational needs.

- **D — Document:** Information is captured in a standardized format with unique identification, ensuring traceability.
- **A — Approve:** Records carry authorized signatures, establishing accountability and authenticity under audit conditions.
- **D — Detain:** Records are retained for legally mandated periods (e.g., 5 years under OSHA, 3 years under Factories Act Sec 88A), creating an evidentiary trail.

**Significance:** Without DAD compliance, organizations lose legal protection, fail statutory audits, and lose institutional safety memory.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> *(a)* Discuss the **purpose, components, and types of Risk Assessment** in industrial safety. *(7 Marks)*
> *(b)* Calculate the **FAR** for a company that had **3 fatal accidents** in a year, employing **600 workers** working **8 hours/day for 300 days/year**. Interpret the result. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Solution — Part (a):**

**Purpose of Risk Assessment:**

1. **Hazard Identification** — Systematically identify what can cause harm.
2. **Risk Estimation** — Evaluate how likely and how severe the harm could be.
3. **Decision Support** — Determine whether risks are acceptable or require treatment.
4. **Prioritization** — Rank risks for resource allocation.
5. **Legal Compliance** — Meet statutory duties (Factories Act Sec 7A, ISO 45001 Clause 6.1).
6. **Continuous Improvement** — Feed into PDCA cycle.

**Components of Risk Assessment:**

- **Hazard Identification**
- **Consequence Analysis**
- **Probability Estimation**
- **Risk Calculation (R = P × S × E)**
- **Risk Evaluation (against criteria)**
- **Documentation in Risk Register**

**Types of Risk Assessment (with applicability):**

| Type | Application |
|---|---|
| Qualitative | Screening, low-complexity tasks |
| Semi-Quantitative | Fine-Kinney Method, risk matrices |
| Quantitative | QRA, chemical process industries |

*(7 marks: ~3 for purpose, ~2 for components, ~2 for types)*

---

**Solution — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**

$$N_f = 3$$
$$\text{Workers} = 600,\ \text{Hours/day} = 8,\ \text{Days/year} = 300$$

**Step 1 — Total Man-Hours**

$$T_h = 600 \times 8 \times 300 = 1{,}440{,}000\ \text{man-hours}$$

**Step 2 — Apply FAR Formula**

$$FAR = \frac{N_f \times 10^8}{T_h} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1{,}440{,}000}$$
$$FAR = \frac{300{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440{,}000} \approx 208.33$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

$$FAR \approx 208$$

This FAR of **208** is **substantially higher than world-class benchmarks (< 1)** and even exceeds the **Indian manufacturing average (100–250)**. The company must undertake immediate safety system review, including process safety management, behavioral safety programs, and management audits.

*(7 marks: 3 for calculation, 2 for interpretation, 2 for recommendation)*

---

### Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Question]**
> *(a)* Explain the **anatomy of an Incident Report**. Discuss its **statutory importance** under the Factories Act, 1948. *(7 Marks)*
> *(b)* Explain the **hierarchy of controls** with a suitable industrial example. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Solution — Part (a):**

**Anatomy of an Incident Report (10 components):**

1. Header — Date, time, location, reporter ID
2. Personnel involved — Names, designation, injury details
3. Description — Factual chronological narrative
4. Witness statements — Verbatim, signed, dated
5. Immediate causes — Unsafe acts/conditions
6. Root causes — Management system failures
7. Injury assessment — Body part, severity
8. Photographs / Sketches — Scene evidence
9. Recommendations — Immediate, short-term, long-term
10. Signatures — Reporter, supervisor, safety officer

**Statutory Importance (Factories Act, 1948):**

- **Section 88A** mandates written notice to the Inspector within prescribed time.
- **Section 111** prescribes penalties for non-reporting.
- **Section 78** mandates medical records post-incident.
- Records serve as **legal evidence** in civil/criminal proceedings and **workmen compensation claims** under the Employees' Compensation Act.

*(7 marks: 4 for anatomy, 3 for statutory importance)*

---

**Solution — Part (b):**

**Hierarchy of Controls (Most to Least Effective):**

1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard.
2. **Substitution** — Replace with less hazardous material/equipment.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Guards, ventilation, interlocks.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Procedures, training, signage.
5. **PPE** — Last line of defense.

**Industrial Example — Solvent-Based Cleaning Operation:**

| Level | Application |
|---|---|
| Elimination | Stop using solvent; switch to aqueous cleaning |
| Substitution | Replace toluene with limonene (less toxic) |
| Engineering | Install local exhaust ventilation (LEV) |
| Administrative | Develop SOP for cleaning; train operators |
| PPE | Issue organic vapor respirators, gloves |

**Conclusion:** Effective safety programs apply multiple control levels simultaneously, never relying on PPE alone.

*(7 marks: 3 for hierarchy explanation, 4 for industrial example)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **❌ Common Mistake 1:** Writing only "Risk = Probability × Severity" — KTU expects the **full Fine-Kinney form (R = P × S × E)** when explicitly asked for "risk calculation." Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **❌ Common Mistake 2:** Omitting the **Exposure (E)** factor when explaining Fine-Kinney. Loss: **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **❌ Common Mistake 3:** Failing to **interpret** FAR/LTIFR values against benchmarks — calculations alone earn only partial credit. Loss: **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **❌ Common Mistake 4:** In risk assessment questions, students often *list* hazards without *evaluating* or *ranking* them. Always score and prioritize. Loss: **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **❌ Common Mistake 5:** Writing about checklists without giving a **structure (header, body, footer)** — examiners expect a layout. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 6. **❌ Common Mistake 6:** In incident report questions, omitting **statutory linkage** (Factories Act Sec 88A) — examiners specifically test this. Loss: **1 mark**.
>
> 7. **❌ Common Mistake 7:** Confusing "**near-miss**" reporting with "**first-aid**" reporting — they have different classifications and statutory implications. Loss: **1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

### 🔑 Key Definitions to Memorize

- ✅ **Safety Documentation:** Controlled, retrievable, auditable written information.
- ✅ **Safety Procedure:** Step-by-step prescribed safe method.
- ✅ **Risk Assessment:** Hazard ID + Risk Analysis + Risk Evaluation.
- ✅ **Incident Report:** Formal factual record of unplanned events.
- ✅ **Checklist:** Structured verification list for tasks/inspections.
- ✅ **Audit:** Systematic, independent, documented examination.

### 🧮 Formulas to Memorize (No Excuses)

| Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|
| $R = P \times S \times E$ | Fine-Kinney Risk Score |
| $FAR = \dfrac{N_f \times 10^8}{T_h}$ | Fatal Accident Rate |
| $LTIFR = \dfrac{LTI \times 10^6}{T_h}$ | Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate |
| $SR = \dfrac{Lost\ Days \times 10^6}{T_h}$ | Severity Rate |
| $RRF = \dfrac{R_{before}}{R_{after}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor |

### 📚 Statutory Provisions (Frequently Asked)

- ✅ Factories Act Sec **7A** — General duties of occupier.
- ✅ Factories Act Sec **11** — Cleanliness.
- ✅ Factories Act Sec **36** — Dangerous fumes.
- ✅ Factories Act Sec **78** — Medical examination.
- ✅ Factories Act Sec **88A** — Notice of accident (reporting).
- ✅ ISO 45001 Clause **7.5.3** — Documented information control.
- ✅ OSHA 29 CFR **1904** — Injury & illness recordkeeping.

### 📊 Hierarchy of Controls (Order is Critical — Do NOT Reverse)

ELIMINATION → SUBSTITUTION → ENGINEERING → ADMINISTRATIVE → PPE

### 🧭 Heinrich's Domino Theory Mapping

Each domino corresponds to a **specific document type** — remember the chain:
*Ancestry → Faulty Person → Unsafe Act/Condition → Accident → Injury*

### 📝 Six Pillars Recap

1. **Safety Procedures** (Prescriptive)
2. **Risk Assessments** (Analytical)
3. **Incident Reports** (Retrospective)
4. **Record Keeping** (Persistent)
5. **Checklists** (Verification)
6. **Audit Documentation** (Evaluative)

### ⚡ One-Line Exam Ready Statements

- *"If it is not documented, it did not happen."*
- *"Documentation transforms tacit safety knowledge into explicit organizational memory."*
- *"A checklist defends against the limits of human memory under stress."*
- *"The hierarchy of controls begins with elimination — never PPE."*
- *"FAR measures fatal risk per 100 million man-hours worked."*
- *"Risk = Probability × Severity × Exposure (Fine-Kinney)."*
- *"Every incident is a near-miss that has already happened."*

### 🎯 KTU Frequently Tested Keywords (For Spotting Questions)

- "Components of…" → List format answer
- "Explain the importance of…" → Definition + advantages + limitations
- "Calculate FAR / LTIFR…" → Formula + substitution + interpretation
- "Discuss in detail…" → Comprehensive 14-mark answer with examples
- "Compare…" → Tabular comparative analysis
- "Apply JSA / HAZOP to..." → Methodology + step-by-step application
- "Discuss the legal provisions…" → Section-wise statutory linkage
